Published Small-N Studies Don’t Matter (and We’re Lucky They Don’t)

Life with Universally Huge Treatment Effects Would Be a Nightmare

Rex W. Douglass PhD — rexdouglass.com — April 15, 2026

A Day in a Life of Huge Treatment Effects

Your morning starts with wild swings in trust for your spouse: up 11% from holding a warm cup of coffee,1 then down 30% from the smell of last night’s kitchen trash.2 Uncertain about your marriage, you pick out a red shirt for the 10% bump on the attractiveness scale3 and head to work. There a power pose jacks your testosterone 30%,4 and putting your feet up on the desk makes you 43% more likely to take a risky bet.5 Your inevitable meeting with HR is all but guaranteed by a dim break-room light that made you cheat on your reimbursement forms 47% above baseline.6 To save your job and protect your health, you duck out of work early and head to the bar. The bottle of red wine reduces your cardiovascular mortality 30% and your all-cause mortality 20%.7 Somehow you make it home and vow to do better the next day. You swallow a fistful of vitamin C,8 echinacea,9 zinc,10 and melatonin,11 all guaranteed to halve various medical and sleep problems. Exhausted, you doze off to a podcast about there being yet another study proving the existence of ESP.12

Huge Silly Treatment Effects are Hallucinations from a Broken Scientific Publishing Industry

Why does science keep popularizing obviously absurd claims about trivial things having large impacts? At least two reasons.

The first you’ve probably heard of: the file drawer. Researchers run many studies and only publish the ones that cross an arbitrary threshold of statistical significance. That’s the diagonal line in Figure 1. Studies below it mostly never appear; studies above it might. The bigger the sample size N (along the horizontal axis), the easier that threshold is to clear.

The second you might not have noticed. Small-N studies cannot produce small-effect publications. The arithmetic won’t allow it. A study with N = 40 has to report d ≥ 0.62 (Cohen’s d—treatment effect over outcome SD) to reach p < 0.05, regardless of what’s actually true about the world. So when small-N studies do publish, they publish huge effects—not because huge effects are there, but because nothing smaller can get through. All six intro findings plotted here sit in the top-left quadrant: unlikely effect sizes propped up by weak evidence.

Figure 1. The p < 0.05 publication boundary. For a two-sample t-test at α = 0.05, a study can only report a significant result if the observed d exceeds 2.77/√(N/2). The shaded boundary traces this minimum across sample sizes. Below it: no p < 0.05 for that simple contrast; under the significance-filter regime, usually no publishable finding. The six hollow points are the findings from the day-in-the-life scenario in the opening section. Dashed lines divide the plot by effect-size likelihood (cut at d = 0.2) and evidence strength (cut at N = 300). The upper-left quadrant—small N, huge reported d—is the regime this essay is about.

It's Not Just the Silly Treatments — Most Huge Effects are Hallucinations

Unfortunately you cannot tell which huge treatment effects to believe or dismiss by how silly the treatment sounds. Researchers pick the most promising studies to replicate and scale up all the time, and they consistently find most of those effects shrink or disappear entirely. Below are 555 pairs of hand-picked studies replicated with a larger-N design.

The pairs come from the major coordinated replication efforts: the Reproducibility Project: Psychology, Many Labs 1–5, the Social Sciences and Experimental Economics Replication Projects, the Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology, DARPA SCORE, the Cova experimental philosophy replication, the Pipeline Project, and the Registered Replication Reports.

Figure 2 plots most of them. Forty-two percent shrank below the p < 0.05 boundary at the replication’s sample size — the original was only publishable because its N was small. The surviving 58% mostly shrank too. Every 10× increase in N cut the reported effect by about two-thirds.

Figure 2. What happens when each of the 555 is replicated at scale. Each arrow connects one originally-published finding (tail) to the same effect re-estimated in a large-N replication (head). Of the 404 arrows shown, 163 (40%) shrink but stay above the p < 0.05 boundary (pink); 170 (42%) shrink below it (red) — meaning the effect would no longer clear significance at the replication N; and 71 (18%) grow (green). The dashed line is the OLS fit: on average, a 10× increase in N is accompanied by a 67% reduction in d. Compare to the cross-sectional slope in Figure 4.

The originals are not approximations to the truth that better statistics could clean up. They are filter artifacts: numbers the publication boundary selected into existence. There’s nothing reanalysis can do. The only remedy is to rerun at large N.

When you read the next small-N paper claiming d = 0.8 from N = 40, this is what you’re looking at.

Most Effect Sizes Must be Small

The replication data gives us something rare: an empirical picture of real effect sizes. Sample sizes are set before the result is known, so the publication filter has no role. What the researchers measure is what the effect is.

Figure 3 shows the distribution. The orange curve is what the 555 originals reported. The black curve is what the replications measured. The black curve is what reality looks like when nobody is straining to find a result: median d = 0.28, most mass under 0.5, a thinning tail.

Figure 3. The 555 replications, before and after. Orange: the distribution of Cohen’s d reported in the 555 original publications that later became replication targets (median d ≈ 0.55). Black: the distribution of the same 555 effects when re-measured at large N in the subsequent replication attempts (median d = 0.28). The gap between the two curves is what the publication filter was doing to those specific findings: compressing an underlying distribution of small effects into a published distribution of large ones. The black curve is the closest thing we have to an empirical picture of what effect sizes actually look like when the p < 0.05 filter is disabled.

The empirical distribution is what it is for reasons. Appendix F spells out eleven mechanisms that each force effects toward zero, across three layers.

Outcomes are bounded. The variables behavioral and biomedical science measure — mood, weight, test scores, blood pressure — have finite variance because they are aggregates over many micro-events, they dissipate energy, they sit inside homeostatic feedback loops, they depend on finite substrates (time, ATP, income), and they are embedded in equilibria that absorb repeated interventions210,208,212.

Given bounded outcomes, the mechanisms that move them must be small:

  • Polygenicity / piranha bound. When k independent causes share a bounded variance budget, the average standardized effect scales as O(k−1/2)153,28,29.
  • Mediation chains attenuate geometrically. Behavioral interventions travel through chains of intermediates; the total standardized effect is the product of the per-link path coefficients158,159.
  • Counterfactual gap against active controls. Effect sizes are properties of contrasts, not of treatments; the better the control, the smaller the gap163,59,164.
  • Spearman reliability ceiling. Observed correlations are bounded by the reliabilities of the measurements that produced them166,167.
  • Ceiling and floor effects. If the outcome can’t go higher, the effect size can’t go higher171,172.

The reporting apparatus then inflates what survives, and pilots don’t scale:

  • Winner’s curse / Type M inflation. Conditioning an estimate on passing |t| > 1.96 inflates its magnitude, even with no journal selection; the inflation is a function of power169,31,170.
  • Hawthorne and demand effects. Lab effects that are really “participants knowing they’re being watched” vanish at scale194,195,193.
  • Heterogeneity absorbs the average. Interventions that help some and harm others give an ATE near zero with large individual effects underneath173,174.
  • Ecological / compositional effects. Group-level correlations aren’t individual-level correlations; the same data can give opposite signs183,184.
  • Site-to-site voltage drop. Even identical pre-registered protocols produce irreducibly different effects across sites20,192.
  • Equilibration. Within a literature, easy wins are harvested first; what remains gets harder179,182.

Three independent empirical literatures then confirm the total size of the resulting gap: coordinated replication projects, large-N replications of specific famous findings, and cross-literature effect-size censuses. The full argument is Appendix F.

A World In Which Most Published Research Was True Would Be A Nightmare Hellscape

Consider the following proof by contradiction. Suppose large effect sizes didn’t require large evidence. Published research was mostly true and could be taken literally. What would that world look like? A 40-person undergraduate study reporting d = 0.8 would be as informative about reality as a 40,000-person preregistered RCT reporting the same thing. The sample size would be a cost consideration, not an epistemic one.

What would the published record look like in that world? Figure 4 plots 13,838 psychology papers (from eight major journals, 1985—2013)13,14 and 272 economics papers (from three independently collected corpora), each at its own N and reported d. No hand-picking—a machine parsed the reported t, F, r, and χ² statements. Eighty-seven percent of psychology papers and seventy-five percent of economics papers sit above the p < 0.05 curve, and most cluster in the small-N, large-d corner.

Figure 4. The general published literature, two disciplines on the same axes. Blue dots: 13,838 psychology papers from eight major psychology journals (1985–2013), each one’s median reported d at its median N, extracted from the statcheck corpus13,14. Green circles: 272 economics papers drawn from three independently collected corpora, each paper plotted at its median derived-d and its median (or recorded) N. 110 are causal-inference articles from the top-25 econ journals (2015–2018), from the Brodeur, Cook & Heyes198 “Methods Matter” replication package (RCT, RDD, DiD, IV tests with reported N). 121 are capital-labor substitution studies from the Gechert–Havránek–Iršová–Kolcunová196 meta-analysis of the production-function elasticity σ. 41 are household-panel studies of the elasticity of intertemporal substitution from the Havránek197 meta-analysis (micro subset, N = individuals). Three reference curves trace where a two-sample t-test at N participants just barely clears p < 0.10, p < 0.05, and p < 0.01. 87% of psychology papers and 75% of economics papers sit above the p < 0.05 curve. Both log–log regression slopes are near −0.45 (−0.45 for psychology, -0.55 for combined economics): a tenfold increase in N is associated with a 64% reduction in reported d in psychology and a 72% reduction in economics. The two fields live at very different sample-size regimes — psychology median N = 61, economics median N varies across its three sub-corpora (Brodeur ≈ 4,800; EIS ≈ 5,700; σ ≈ 44) — but all produce the same boundary-hugging scatter along a similar slope. The filter is doing the same work across independently collected corpora, which is difficult to reconcile with a “psychology’s specific problem” interpretation and easy to reconcile with a publication-filter interpretation that is field-agnostic.

A frequentist defender would object here. A p < 0.05 finding isn’t claimed with certainty; the threshold itself admits a 1-in-20 false-positive rate, and p < 0.01 admits 1-in-100. Discount the published effects by those rates before taking them literally. Fine. Discount at 5% and roughly 12,500 of the 14,110 papers still represent real large effects. Discount at 1% and it’s still about 13,970. The false-positive rate doesn’t dent the arithmetic at this scale. And the p-value discount is about whether an effect exists under the null, not about whether the reported magnitude is right — so even a hypothetical literature with zero false positives would still have to justify the claimed d.

Take those papers at face value. Every point above the curve is a real large effect. That’s roughly 12,000 significant psychology findings in this corpus alone, most of them reporting d between 0.3 and 2.0, from studies with 30 to 200 participants. Each describes an intervention — a prime, a scale, a framing, a brief instruction — that moves a human outcome by a substantial fraction of a standard deviation.

Multiply it out. If every published finding in this corpus were a real large effect, behavioral science would have discovered tens of thousands of vaccine-scale interventions on human cognition, judgment, and behavior over three decades. A typical psychology graduate program would be producing several such discoveries per year. The world would be rebuilt by them.

It isn’t. None of these interventions has transformed a classroom, a clinic, a marketplace, or a voting booth at the magnitudes claimed. The claimed effects don’t show up at scale, don’t replicate under preregistration, don’t survive contact with active controls. The published record is consistent with a world in which large effect sizes are common and easy to find. The actual world is not that one. So large effect sizes must require large evidence — more than a 40-person study can produce.

Because Large Effects Are Special, They Require Special Evidence

Real large effects exist. What makes them real is that they don’t need 42-person studies to see them.

Smallpox killed roughly 30% of the people it infected; vaccination drove case incidence to zero within the target populations, a transformation visible in uncontrolled 18th-century observation. Measles vaccination reduced US incidence by more than 99%, visible in national surveillance within years of introduction. The mRNA COVID-19 vaccines reported ~95% efficacy against symptomatic infection in phase 3 RCTs with tens of thousands of participants per arm. Penicillin turned bacterial pneumonia from a disease with roughly 30% case-fatality into one with roughly 5%.

These effects were not found by 40-person lab studies. They were found by their targets collapsing. Crude measurement sufficed. No p-value ritual was required. The effect replicated across populations. There was a specific mechanism — a specific virus, a specific bacterial cell wall, a specific immune response — rather than a vague prime.

Figure 5 shows why this matters for reading the literature. The red line is a flat prior: every effect size from d = 0.01 to d = 1,000 treated as equally plausible before the data arrives. Null-hypothesis significance testing behaves as if this prior were reasonable. The blue curve is Gelman’s weakly informative Normal(0, 0.35) — allows large effects, but treats them as rare. Real large effects sit out in the blue curve’s thin tail. Vaccines, antibiotics, and the handful of other interventions that changed history live there.

Figure 5. What you implicitly believe before the data. Red: the implicit frequentist prior, which treats every possible effect size as equally plausible (including d = 100 and d = 1,000). Blue: Gelman’s weakly informative prior for psychology-sized effects, Normal(0, 0.35), which puts 95% of its mass in |d| < 0.7. Which you pick determines how much to shrink a published small-N finding under Bayesian updating. The first prior shrinks nothing. The second shrinks a published d = 0.8 at N = 30 to a posterior near d = 0.4.

The question the rest of this essay keeps asking: when a 40-person undergraduate study reports d = 0.8 from a two-minute intervention, which prior are you reading the claim under? Under the flat prior, it’s plausible. Under the Gelman prior, it’s a rare-event claim — and rare-event claims need rare-event evidence. Vaccine-scale effects came with hospital wards emptying, populations visibly transformed, case-fatality statistics collapsing. A t-test on 40 undergraduates is not that.

A world where everything mattered would be terrifying

That life is one in which your fate is dictated by trivia, most of it outside your control. A warm cup, a stray smell, an overhead bulb — each a lever big enough to swing trust, honesty, testosterone, or the odds of a heart attack. Keeping a marriage, a job, or a working cardiovascular system would be a daily gauntlet: meticulous planning to control the inputs, and persistent luck when you couldn’t. No equilibrium you built over years of practice, effort, or cooperation would be stable, because any small thing could knock it over. Choosing the wrong shirt could cost you the promotion. Sitting in the wrong chair could cost you the deal. The right cup of coffee could save your marriage. The wrong one could end it.

And if every small thing mattered, then in another sense nothing would. You couldn’t decide what was worth your attention, because the list of things with life-sized consequences would be infinite. You couldn’t learn from experience, because each day’s outcome would be dominated by a different lurking confounder. You couldn’t give advice to your children, because whatever rule you landed on would be overturned by the next trivial intervention to come down the pike. A world in which everything is a huge treatment effect is a world in which agency, planning, and accumulated knowledge collapse into noise.

Mysticisms about extreme agency and helplessness are common throughout human culture

Humans keep inventing this worldview. In every era, in every culture, some version of it shows up: your fate is dictated by forces outside your control — the stars, the gods, karma, ancestors, trauma, your microbiome, your vibration — and also every small thing you do is loaded with ultimate consequence201. You are simultaneously helpless and supernaturally responsible, and the contradiction is the point; a doctrine that resolved cleanly into either fatalism or agency would lose half its customers. The meme is stable because it pays two constituencies at once. For the individual it is the most flattering theory of the self ever invented: when things go well, you worked the system; when things go badly, the system (or karma202, or someone else’s negative energy) worked you. Vague enough personality claims feel personally true to almost everyone203. For predatory institutions the same structure is a revenue model, because if every small thing matters and the mechanisms are invisible, the list of fixes someone can sell you is infinite. Religions sold indulgences on this logic for centuries207; prosperity preachers sell seed-faith donations on it now204; a $6.8-trillion global wellness industry sells supplements, cleanses, and energy work205; MLM uplines sell the dream that the right mindset produces the right income, and most participants make little or no money doing it206. The product varies; the sales pitch is identical — there is a hidden lever, you have been pulling it wrong, and I can show you how. The pitch only works in a world of huge treatment effects. In a world of small, noisy, hard-won effects, no one needs a guru.

Science’s version of this mysticism is small-N frequentism (a cult of the naive and the criminally negligent)

The published scientific literature has its own version of the same meme. A graduate student runs 42 undergraduates through a two-minute posture manipulation and reports that the treatment raised testosterone by 30% and risk tolerance by 45%. A lab runs 67 undergraduates past a plate of radishes and reports that willpower is a depletable resource that cookies consume. A paper runs 30 students through a scrambled-sentence prime and reports that reading words about old age makes people walk 15% slower down a hallway. Each study has a tiny sample, a tiny intervention, and a huge reported effect. Each was published in a prestigious journal. Each produced a decade of follow-up research, popular-press coverage, TED talks, management consulting, and policy recommendations. The structure is the same as the religious version: a small, nearly invisible lever produces an enormous, life-sized consequence. The statistics are different from magic, but the shape of the claim is not.

What a flat prior commits you to

Strictly speaking, NHST does not have a prior. That is part of the problem. A p-value is not (P(H_0 | data)), and a confidence interval is not born as a posterior distribution over the true effect. But when readers convert a significant estimate and its confidence interval into belief about the true effect, the object they are tacitly using is the flat-prior posterior in disguise. In the normal approximation, δ̂ | δ ∼ N(δ, s²); combine that likelihood with the improper prior π(δ) ∝ 1 and the posterior is δ | δ̂ ∼ N(δ̂, s²) — the same Gaussian bell that the confidence distribution traces. That is the no-shrinkage prior: not a probability distribution, not normalizable, and perfectly happy to assign equal density to equal-width intervals around d = 0.2, d = 5, and d = 1,000. No one believes this out loud. Figure 5 draws what the belief would look like. The publication system behaves as if authors and reviewers were doing exactly that calculation.

NHST contains no prior at all. The flat prior appears when readers read a significant estimate and its confidence interval as a belief distribution over the true effect—which is how unshrunk point estimates routinely get read. With that implicit flat prior, a significant p-value at n = 30 is treated as evidence for an effect. A reasonable prior — one that puts, say, 95% of its mass in |d| < 0.7, because that’s where psychology-sized effects actually live — would demand far more data before allowing the same conclusion. The flat prior removes this brake, and the consequence is the gamification of discovery: instead of asking “how much data do I need to detect a psychologically plausible effect,” a researcher asks “how little data can I get away with and still clear the threshold.” The answer is often remarkably little — thirty subjects, if the effect you report is big enough. Coupled with the publication filter, where journals print the papers that clear p < 0.05 and not the ones that don’t, you end up with a literature that consists almost entirely of studies that had to report implausibly large effects in order to exist, and almost none that reported the actual small effects that were there. The published record contains the untrue things and excludes the true ones. Figures 1 and 5 together are the mechanism. Figure 6 is what the mechanism produces.

Figure 6. The general published literature, as a distribution of effect sizes. Each paper in the Figure 4 scatter contributes one value: its median reported Cohen’s d. The distribution peaks at d ≈ 0.5 with median 0.72. When the reported test statistics are converted to Cohen’s d and collapsed to paper-level medians, 95% of the 14,110 papers fall at or above d = 0.2 (what Cohen called a “small” effect), 72% at or above d = 0.5 (“medium”), and 43% at or above d = 0.8 (“large”). Source corpora: 13,838 psychology papers from the Nuijten et al. statcheck extraction covering eight major psychology journals (1985–2013), 272 paper-level economics effect estimates — a convenience subset with non-missing N from the Brodeur–Cook–Heyes, Gechert–HavrÃ¡nek–IršovÃ¡–KolcunovÃ¡, and HavrÃ¡nek EIS micro economics corpora.

What the published distribution actually looks like

If you believe any one of the 14,110 papers in Figure 6 is a literal reading of the effect size in the world, you have to believe all 14,110 of them. They were produced by the same process, vetted by the same journals, and cited by the same kinds of people. Ninety-five percent claim an effect at least as large as a small one; 43% claim an effect larger than what Cohen called large. If that distribution were real — if it described how the world actually works — you would live in a world where tens of thousands of huge effects are being discovered every day. Every semester, a psychology graduate student would find that their two-minute manipulation produced a life-altering shift in their subjects’ behavior. Every month, an economist would discover an intervention that moved a macroeconomic quantity by half a standard deviation. Every year, a new field would emerge from another round of Cohen’s-d-bigger-than-one findings, with another round of TED talks and management consultants. You do not live in that world. Nobody lives in that world. The distribution in Figure 6 is not a distribution of real effects; it is the distribution that the publication filter was always going to produce, because it is what the filter was set up to produce.

A Sane Belief System that Matches Our Actual World

By “small-N studies don’t matter” I mean the thing that deserves contempt: underpowered, one-shot, between-person studies of noisy behavioral, biomedical, or social outcomes, especially when they report large effects from tiny interventions. I do not mean within-subject psychophysics where one subject contributes thousands of trials, single-gene knockouts in model organisms, or any setting where the signal-to-noise ratio is high enough that N = 10 has real resolving power. Those are not the cult. The cult is 42 people, one weird prime, one p-value, and a civilization-scale conclusion. The rest of this essay is about the cult.

The solution is not to work harder at running small-N studies. It is to change what you believe before you look at the data. The frequentist prior in Figure 5 is what makes Figure 6 possible. A prior that reflects the world we actually live in — a world where real effects are usually small, where large effects are rare and usually restricted to interventions that are obvious without a study — shrinks small-N findings automatically, before any question of p-value or publication arises.

The Gelman prior is a reasonable place to start — reasonable in the literal sense, that a reasonable person, told in advance what behavioral-science effect sizes look like, would write down something approximately like Normal(0, 0.35). The replication evidence shown earlier (Figures 2 and 3) is where this prior comes from empirically: the post-replication distribution of effect sizes sits almost exactly where the Gelman prior said it would.

Objections

Four objections are worth taking seriously. None of them saves the literature this essay is about.

“But there are exceptions where effects really are large.”

Appendix B catalogs the exceptions in detail. The taxonomy is not alphabetical and not by academic field; it is by architectural type. Infectious disease control. Deficiency replacement. Mechanical and structural interventions. A handful of cardiovascular and metabolic pharmacologic classes. A short list of psychiatric interventions with clear mechanistic targets. A few educational interventions that happen to combine intensity, targeting, and a weak counterfactual. Behavioral policy interventions that change defaults rather than minds. Agricultural and environmental interventions that address a genuine limiting factor.

What these share is the shape of the compound model with each factor pushed favorably. A dominant causal bottleneck means k is effectively 1, so 1/√k is not small. A short causal chain means the product of path coefficients is close to 1. A large counterfactual gap means the control arm’s shared-ingredient fraction is close to 0. A well-measured proximal outcome means rxx is close to 1. When all four structural factors are favorable and the intervention is tested rigorously enough that the selection filter does no work, the effect can be enormous. Antibiotics against susceptible pathogens produce d-equivalents that do not fit on normal charts. Insulin moved life expectancy in Type 1 diabetes from months to decades. Cataract surgery restores sight.

The pattern of exceptions is the strongest possible confirmation of the rule. Large effects require specific structural conditions. Those conditions are rare. Their rarity is why most effects are small. Interventions that meet the full checklist are not distributed uniformly across human problems; they cluster in a few therapeutic areas (infection, deficiency, mechanical dysfunction) and a few behavioral domains (default architecture, choice of instruction intensity). Outside those clusters, the expected effect of any intervention is small, and researchers who report large effects should be read as having either (a) found a rare genuine exception that satisfies the checklist, or (b) produced an estimate that will not survive contact with replication, scale-up, or bias correction.

“It’s just p-hacking. Fix the filter and the rest takes care of itself.”

Fix every form of p-hacking tomorrow. A two-arm behavioral study with N = 40 still crosses p < 0.05 on an observed d of roughly 0.62, still has 23% power against a true d = 0.4, still selects upper-tail draws from its sampling distribution. Type M inflation on a small-N significant finding is approximately 2× under fully honest analysis—a property of the sampling distribution, not of researcher behavior.

Registered Reports make this concrete. Scheel, Schijen, and Lakens17 compared 71 preregistered Registered Reports to 152 standard articles and found positive-result rates of 96% versus 44%—a 52 percentage-point drop once p-hacking is removed by design. But 44% of honestly-analyzed studies still cross significance, in a world where most true effects are under d = 0.25. Those positive findings are inflated upper-tail draws. They still don’t replicate cleanly.

The filter is not the primary disease. The primary disease is that the resolution of the instrument doesn’t match the size of the object being measured. Remove p-hacking and you trade a literature of inflated positives for a literature of honest small-sample noise plus the rare large effect. Cleaner, but not informative.

“Researchers calibrate sample size to the true effect.”

This is the anthropic principle: investigators have priors about effect sizes, run power analyses, and size their N accordingly. A published N = 40 reflects an investigator who had reason to expect a detectable effect at that sample.

The best version of this objection is van Zwet, Gelman, and Więcek218, whose model of 80,000 studies across 11 curated corpora includes exactly this claim. Their data refute it.

Across their corpora—Cochrane, clinical trials, ecology, political science, exercise research, economics, psychology, psychotherapy, education, intelligence—3–11% of studies achieve 80% probability of significance. Not eighty percent of studies. Three to eleven. Political science: 7%. Cochrane: 10%. Exercise and cognition: 3%. The modal published study is radically underpowered for the effect it is nominally detecting. vZGW’s own interpretation is that researchers size studies “just about large enough to assess the direction of the effect of interest, but not larger”—under pressures of time, money, and logistics.

That is not calibration. It is the minimum sample required to cross the filter on a favorable noise draw, which is exactly what the rest of this essay has argued. The anthropic principle is real; it does not say what the objection needs it to say. It says researchers size for filter-crossing, not for detection.

“At least the sign is right.”

The sharpest version of this objection is vZGW218. Their model reports that published significant findings carry the correct sign 82–96% of the time at |z| = 1.96 and 94–99% at |z| ≥ 1.96, across all 11 of their curated corpora. Their interpretation: “empirical research often leads to true findings—in the sense of correctly identifying the direction of an effect—that do not replicate in the sense of achieving statistically significant result in the same direction as the original study.” Magnitudes are inflated; direction is informative; the replication crisis is overstated.

This is wrong, and the way it is wrong is instructive.

The numbers are not a measurement. They are the output of a model. vZGW fit a mixture of half-normals to the observed |z| distribution in each corpus, apply a Hedges single-parameter correction for publication bias, deconvolve out a standard normal noise component, and read off a posterior distribution over the true SNR. The 82–96% correct-sign figure is what that posterior implies under the model’s assumptions. It is not the result of comparing published signs to any external ground truth.

The model’s central assumption fails in exactly the subfields where the crisis lives. The assumption is that observed z equals true SNR plus standard normal noise. For this to hold, the published estimate must be an unbiased draw from its sampling distribution. That assumption holds when the study is well-identified: the construct is measured cleanly, the intervention is delivered as described, selection into the sample is exogenous, the analysis is fixed in advance. In that regime, upper-tail draws that cross the filter are disproportionately sign-concordant with the true effect, because the true mean and forward-direction noise point the same way. Correct sign 90% of the time falls out.

The assumption does not hold when the research process itself has degrees of freedom correlated with direction—specification selection, construct misspecification, demand characteristics, identification strategies that don’t identify what they claim. In that regime, the “noise” is not symmetric around the true effect. It is drawn from the space of specifications the researcher was willing to report. The filter still selects upper-tail draws, but those draws align with what the researcher expected to find, not with reality. A field that operates on a wrong shared prior produces a literature full of findings pointing in the theory-predicted direction, regardless of whether the theory is correct. vZGW’s model reads the resulting internal consistency as “high correct-sign rate.” It is not. It is internal consistency with a wrong prior.

The direct empirical test defeats the model where it matters. vZGW’s model does not have access to the true sign of the effect. Large preregistered replications do, at least approximately, because they restore the conditions under which observed z really is an unbiased draw. Run the test:

  • Chabris et al. 2012219: 12 previously-published candidate-IQ-gene effects tested in roughly 10,000 subjects. Replicated: zero. A coin produces six. Published sign correct rate: 0%.
  • Duncan and Keller 201180: 103 novel psychiatric G×E findings, 10 direct replications, 27% replication rate, pair-level sign match near 50%. Coin-equivalent.
  • The Registered Replication Report program and Many Labs 3: ego depletion, facial feedback, professor priming, elderly priming, warmth priming—in aggregate, sign concordance with originals in the 40–60% range.

In the subfields where the replication crisis actually landed—candidate genes, social priming, much of classical social psychology, the first decade of behavioral genetics—the empirical sign concordance between original small-N findings and large-N replications is at or below chance. vZGW’s model predicts 82–96%. Reality delivers 0–50%.

This is not a “different subfield” problem. It is a model-specification problem. vZGW’s framework cannot distinguish a literature that gets the sign right 90% of the time from a literature that is internally consistent 90% of the time in the direction of a wrong theory. Their model assumes the first; their data are compatible with either; the fields where the assumption is testable show the second. Their optimism is what their model outputs when their assumption is satisfied. In the fields where that optimism would actually matter, the assumption fails.

The replication crisis is not about magnitudes being imprecise while directions remain true. It is about the research process in specific subfields producing published literatures that do not carry reliable information about reality—not about magnitude, not about sign. vZGW have described a mathematical property of their model. They have not described the literature.

What this means for reading research

Carry the empirical distribution into every paper. When a behavioral or social science study reports d > 0.5, that estimate sits above the 90th percentile of its field’s real distribution. It is not obviously wrong, but it is not obviously right either. Ask whether the intervention plausibly satisfies enough of the structural checklist to justify its position in the tail. Ask whether the sample was large enough for the estimate to be informative about anything other than the sampling distribution. Ask whether the outcome was pre-specified. Ask whether the comparator was a placebo or something closer to active care. Ask whether the study’s authors have a credible record of similar-magnitude effects surviving replication.

When an observational study in medicine or epidemiology reports an odds ratio of 3 or a hazard ratio of 0.4, default to skepticism. Those magnitudes are rare in the experimental literature and common in the confounded one. The class of claims large enough to be clinically transformative but small enough to be within observational reach is narrow; most claims in that range are confounded, and Mendelian randomization, instrumental variables, or RCTs where available tend to attenuate or reverse them.

Small, precisely estimated effects from large preregistered studies with active controls are more credible than large, imprecisely estimated effects from small exploratory ones — and they are more predictive of real-world impact when interventions are scaled. The question to carry into every paper is not “is this significant?” but “is this effect size plausible given the empirical distribution of effects in this field, the structural features of this intervention, and the methodological strength of this study?”

What this means for doing research

Design studies for the effect sizes that exist. A study powered to detect d = 0.50 in a field whose real effects cluster at 0.15 is powered to detect only the most inflated claims, and will — conditional on producing a significant result — contribute mostly to the right tail of the literature rather than to its center. Sample sizes must match the empirical distribution, which means they must often be larger than current norms by one to two orders of magnitude.

Preregister the primary analysis. Use active controls where the counterfactual is otherwise ill-defined. Measure outcomes reliably enough that the rxx ceiling is not the binding constraint. Report bias-corrected estimates alongside naive ones when meta-analyzing. Treat a surprising effect size as a prompt to investigate the DGP rather than to celebrate the finding. None of this is methodological luxury; it is the minimum required to contribute signal rather than noise to a literature that already has too much of the latter.

The deepest implication of the small-effects world is that most research, honestly done, will produce small effects, and that most interventions, honestly scaled, will produce small average changes on the outcomes they target. This is not an indictment of research or of intervention. Small effects, applied at scale across populations and compounded across exposures, are how most of the genuine progress in public health, education, and development has been made. Blood pressure lowered by 2 mmHg prevents thousands of strokes. A few-percent reduction in smoking compounds to enormous lifetime mortality differences. A small per-year effect of a good teacher compounds to hundreds of thousands of dollars in lifetime earnings across a classroom of students. Small d is not small consequence. It is the actual scale at which real interventions on real outcomes operate, once the filter is removed and the counterfactual is honestly specified.

Knowing this changes what you are looking for when you read, what you are willing to publish when you write, and what you expect when you plan. It is not a posture of skepticism. It is the default prior the empirical record demands.

The real distribution — effect sizes across science

This appendix compiles the quantitative evidence underlying the claim that most treatment effects are small. It draws on replication projects, meta-analyses of meta-analyses, government-scale RCT databases, Cochrane reviews, and meta-research studies of publication bias. Within each field, the organizing question is the same: what does the distribution of true effects actually look like, once we correct for the filters between reality and what ends up in print?

Appendix A — The real distribution (you are here) A.1 Metrics· A.2 Headline numbers· A.3 Psychology· A.4 Medicine· A.5 Surgery· A.6 Alt medicine· A.7 Mental health· A.8 Epidemiology· A.9 Genetics· A.10 Neuroscience· A.11 Economics· A.12 Development econ· A.13 Education· A.14 Political science· A.15 Criminology· A.16 Sociology· A.17 Organizational· A.18 Marketing· A.19 Environmental· A.20 Sports science· A.21 Agriculture· A.22 COVID-19· A.23 Publication bias· A.24 Many-analysts· A.25 Obs→exp reversals· A.26 WEIRD samples· A.27 Time trajectory Appendix B — When the world gives you large effects B.1 Infectious disease· B.2 Deficiency replacement· B.3 Mechanical/structural· B.4 Cardiovascular· B.5 Mental health exceptions· B.6 Education exceptions· B.7 Policy architecture· B.8 Agriculture· B.9 Management· B.10 What exceptions share Appendix C — The hellscape catalog C.1 Social priming· C.2 Self-regulation· C.3 Stereotype effects· C.4 Candidate genes

A.1   Metrics and what counts as “small”

Most of what follows uses three metrics. Cohen’s d (and its near-equivalents Hedges’ g, SMD) expresses a group difference in standard deviations. Pearson’s r expresses an association as a correlation coefficient. For small effects, r ≈ d/2; so d = 0.20 corresponds to r ≈ 0.10. For binary outcomes, odds ratios, risk ratios, and hazard ratios express multiplicative effects on event rates.

Cohen’s original benchmarks — d = 0.20 small, 0.50 medium, 0.80 large; r = 0.10 small, 0.30 medium, 0.50 large — were proposed in 1988 as rough guides, not empirical claims. Four decades of meta-research have shown them to be systematically optimistic for most of social and behavioral science. Empirically calibrated benchmarks vary by field:

Source Field Small (25th) Medium (50th) Large (75th)
Cohen32 Proposed universally r=0.10 r=0.30 r=0.50
Gignac & Szodorai33 Individual differences (708 meta-analytic r’s) r=0.11 r=0.19 r=0.29
Lovakov & Agadullina34 Social psychology (12,170 r’s, 134 meta-analyses) r=0.12 r=0.24 r=0.41
Funder & Ozer35 Psychology, recommended r=0.05 r=0.20 r=0.30
Doucouliagos36 Economics (22,000 partial r’s) 0.07 0.17 0.33
Kraft37 Education RCTs d<0.05 d=0.05–0.20 d≥0.20

Cohen’s “medium” of r = 0.30 is the 75th percentile of social psychology and above the 90th percentile of education RCTs. His “large” of r = 0.50 is functionally absent from published meta-analyses of interventions in most applied fields. When this appendix refers to an effect as “small,” it means small relative to the empirical distribution in that field, not relative to Cohen’s out-of-calibration benchmarks.

A.2   Headline numbers, one row per field

The table below is the executive summary of this appendix. Each row is a claim about the typical effect size in a field, with the source that supplies the number. Subsequent sections unpack each row.

Field Typical effect Metric Source
Social psychology r = 0.24 (median) correlation Lovakov & Agadullina34
Psychology, replications d → 0.15–0.20 SMD after replication OSC 2015; Many Labs 2; SSRP 2018; SCORE 2026
Clinical medicine (all Cochrane RCTs) median power 13% typical exaggeration ≈ 3× van Zwet, Schwab & Senn31
Pharmacotherapy, all drugs vs. placebo SMD = 0.37–0.41 median Hedges’ g Leucht et al.38
Oncology drugs, median OS gain 2.1 months overall survival Fojo et al.
Psychotherapy vs. no treatment d = 0.70–0.80 SMD Wampold39
Psychotherapy vs. active control d = 0.20–0.30 SMD Cuijpers et al.40
Brain-behavior (reproducible BWAS) r = 0.01 correlation Marek et al.30
Individual genetic variants (GWAS) <0.1% variance each per-SNP R² Visscher et al.; Yengo et al.
Academic nudge studies 8.7 pp percentage points DellaVigna & Linos22
Same nudges at government scale 1.4 pp (6× shrinkage) percentage points DellaVigna & Linos22
Development interventions d ≈ 0.12 SMD Vivalt41
Education RCTs median d = 0.10; weighted mean 0.04 SMD Kraft37
Campaign persuasion effects ≈ 0 percentage points Kalla & Broockman42
Hot-spots policing d = 0.13 SMD Braga et al.43
Nutritional epidemiology (meta-analyses) RR ≈ 0.96 relative risk Schoenfeld & Ioannidis44
Personnel selection (cognitive ability) r = 0.31 validity coefficient Sackett et al.45
Digital advertising on sales (R²) ≈ 0.000005 individual-level R² Lewis & Rao46
Carbon pricing on emissions −5 to −21% % emissions Döbbeling-Hildebrandt et al.47
Nitrogen fertilizer on crop yield +30 to +75% % yield Global meta-analyses
Insecticide-treated bednets on child mortality RR = 0.83 (17% reduction) relative risk Cochrane
Part I — Field by field

A.3   Psychology

Empirical benchmarks across the whole field

Richard, Bond, and Stokes-Zoota48 synthesized 322 meta-analyses covering one century of social psychology — roughly 25,000 studies and 8 million participants — and reported a mean effect of r = 0.21 with a median of 0.18. Fewer than 24% of effects reached Cohen’s “medium” threshold of r = 0.30. Lovakov and Agadullina34 updated this using 12,170 correlations from 134 meta-analyses, with 25th/50th/75th percentiles at 0.12/0.24/0.41. Gignac and Szodorai33, examining 708 individual-differences correlations, found percentiles at 0.11/0.19/0.29, with fewer than 3% of correlations reaching Cohen’s “large” of r = 0.50. Funder and Ozer35 argued that r ≥ 0.40 should be viewed with suspicion as likely inflated.

Replication projects

Project Scope Original Replicated Pass rate
Open Science Collaboration19 100 psych studies r=0.403 r=0.197 36%
Many Labs 220 28 effects, 125 samples, N=15,305 d=0.60 d=0.15 50–54%
SSRP49 21 Nature/Science studies — ~50% of original 62%
Exp. Economics RP50 18 AER/QJE lab studies — 66% of original 61%
SCORE21 274 claims, 164 papers, 12 disciplines r=0.25 r=0.10 55%

Famous failed and severely shrunken effects

Effect Original Replication Shrinkage
Ego depletion d=0.62 d=0.04 (23 labs, N=2,141) ~94%
Facial feedback (pen-in-mouth) 0.82 units 0.03 units (17 labs) ~96%
Elderly-walking priming (Bargh) d=0.82–1.08 d≈0 ~100%
Power posing (hormonal effects) significant p>0.16 (Ranehill) ~100%
Money priming d=0.34–0.75 d=−0.02 (36 labs) ~100%
Warm cup → warm personality r=0.28–0.31 r≈0 (N=861) ~100%
Implicit bias (IAT) → behavior r=0.274 r=0.148 (Oswald meta) ~46%
Growth mindset interventions d=0.0851 d=0.02 (highest-quality, Macnamara 2023) ~75%
Stereotype threat d>0.5 d=0.22–0.26 meta; possibly null after bias correction 50–100%

Anchoring is the exception. Numerical anchoring on judgments replicates consistently and at substantial magnitudes (d ≈ 0.4–0.8). It is the rare social priming effect that survives. Its survival may tell us something about the others: anchoring operates on an explicit numerical judgment, a short causal chain with a clean measurement. Most failed priming effects asked whether an incidental cue changed a distant behavior through an inferred cognitive route.

Noncognitive skills: grit, mindset, deliberate practice

Grit (Credé, Tynan & Harms52 (2017); 88 samples, N = 66,807): population-corrected correlation with academic performance is ρ = 0.18. Grit correlates ρ = 0.84 with conscientiousness, and after controlling for conscientiousness its incremental validity is β = −0.02 — null.

Growth mindset: the correlation with achievement is r ≈ 0.09 (about 1% of variance). Intervention effects are d = 0.08 overall51, declining to d = 0.05 in Macnamara & Burgoyne53 (63 studies, N = 97,672), and d = 0.02 in the six highest-quality studies. After publication-bias correction, effects are non-significant. Studies by authors with financial conflicts of interest reported significantly larger effects.

Deliberate practice (Macnamara, Hambrick & Oswald54; 88 studies): practice explains 26% of variance in games, 21% in music, 18% in sports, 4% in education, and less than 1% in professional performance. Among elite performers specifically, practice explains about 1%. Ericsson’s “10,000 hours” substantially overstated the contribution.

Cognitive training and far transfer

Sala et al.55 conducted a second-order meta-analysis across 233 comparisons spanning all cognitive training domains. Once placebo effects and publication bias were controlled, the overall far-transfer effect was zero, with zero between-study variance. Near transfer exists (g ≈ 0.37–0.72), but far transfer to fluid intelligence, academic achievement, or real-world performance is consistently null. Owen et al.56 tested over 11,000 participants on online brain training and found no transfer.

Clinical psychology and psychotherapy

See section A.7.

A.4   Medicine & pharmacology

The Cochrane baseline

Van Zwet, Schwab, and Senn31 analyzed 23,551 RCTs from the Cochrane Database and found median statistical power of only 13%. At this power level, significant results overestimate true effects by approximately a factor of 3; even a just-significant result (p = 0.05) overestimates by about 70%. About 88% of RCTs have power below 80%.

Leucht et al.38 compared drug efficacy across 94 meta-analyses and found median SMDs of 0.37 for general medicine and 0.41 for psychiatric drugs — small by Cohen’s benchmarks, and indistinguishable between fields. Leucht et al.57 subsequently found that only 11 of 17 commonly used medications demonstrated a minimal clinically important difference.

Pereira, Horwitz, and Ioannidis58 asked how often medical interventions produce very large effects (OR ≥ 5). Across 85,002 forest plots from 3,082 Cochrane reviews, roughly 10% of first-published trials reported very large effects, but these almost always came from small studies with fewer than 20 events. Only one intervention across the entire Cochrane database — extracorporeal oxygenation for neonates — demonstrated a very large effect on mortality with high-quality supporting evidence.

Drug-class summary

Intervention Comparator Effect Source
Antidepressants (21 drugs) Placebo SMD=0.30 Cipriani et al.59 (522 RCTs, N=116,477)
Antidepressants, FDA vs. published — Published inflated ~32% Turner et al.27
Antipsychotics (schizophrenia) Placebo SMD=0.47; NNT=5 for minimal response Huhn et al.60; Leucht
Anxiolytics (SSRI/SNRI/benzo, GAD) Placebo g=0.37 Bandelow et al.
Methylphenidate (ADHD core symptoms) Placebo SMD=−0.78 (large) Multiple meta-analyses
Paracetamol (back pain) Placebo Zero Machado et al.61
NSAIDs (spinal pain) Placebo Clinically unimportant Machado et al.62
Ibuprofen 400mg (acute pain) Placebo NNT=2.5 Moore et al.
Statins (primary prev., low risk) Placebo NNT=167 over 5 years CTT Collaboration
Statins (secondary prev.) Placebo NNT=19–21 over 10 years CTT Collaboration
Antihypertensives, per 10 mmHg SBP — RR=0.80 for CVD events Ettehad et al.63
SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin) Placebo HR=0.62 CV death EMPA-REG OUTCOME
GLP-1 agonists (liraglutide) Placebo HR=0.87 MACE LEADER trial
Oncology drugs (median OS gain) Control 2.1 months Fojo et al.
Cancer drugs at EMA approval — 57% lacked evidence of OS/QoL benefit Davis et al.64
Nicotine replacement therapy Placebo OR=1.37–1.41 for quit Cochrane
Varenicline Placebo OR=2.33 for quit Cochrane
Naltrexone (alcohol, heavy drinking) Placebo NNT=12 Jonas et al.65
Opioid agonist treatment (mortality) Out of treatment Rate ratio ~3.2× lower Sordo et al.66

Preclinical to clinical. Sena et al.67 showed publication bias inflates preclinical animal stroke effects by ~31%. The Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology24 replicated 50 experiments and found replication effects 85% smaller on average, with 40% replication rate. Roughly 5% of animal-tested therapeutic interventions obtain human regulatory approval25 — a useful anchor for the magnitude of the preclinical-to-clinical translation gap.

A.5   Surgery & procedures

Sham-controlled surgical trials have produced some of modern medicine’s most striking null findings. Jonas et al.68 meta-analyzed 39 studies comparing real surgery to sham procedures and found an overall SMD of 0.34, falling to 0.13 (non-significant) for pain-related conditions. Sham-surgery patients achieved 78% of the improvement seen in active surgery groups for pain outcomes.

Procedure Trial Finding
Arthroscopic knee surgery (OA) Moseley et al.69, N=180 Zero vs. sham
Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy Sihvonen et al. (FIDELITY) 2013, N=146 Zero vs. sham at 2y and 5y
PCI for stable angina ORBITA 2018, N=200 +16.6 s exercise time (NS)
PCI monotherapy for stable angina ORBITA-2 2023, N=301 +59.5 s — about 1 antianginal med
Invasive vs. conservative strategy, stable CAD ISCHEMIA 2020, N=5,179 No difference in composite CV outcome
Vertebroplasty (spinal fractures) Multiple blinded RCTs Null vs. sham

A review of 53 sham-controlled surgical trials found sham surgery as effective as real surgery in more than half. Because most surgical trials cannot be blinded, the published surgical literature is systematically inflated relative to what properly controlled designs reveal.

A.6   Alternative & complementary medicine

Acupuncture (Vickers et al. individual patient meta-analysis, 39 RCTs, 20,827 patients): acupuncture vs. sham acupuncture shows SMDs of 0.15–0.23; acupuncture vs. no treatment shows 0.42–0.57. About 75% of the total effect is attributable to non-specific (placebo) factors; roughly 25% is the “specific” needling effect.

Homeopathy (Shang et al.70, 110 trials): the eight largest, highest-quality trials yielded OR = 0.88 (95% CI 0.66–1.18, p = 0.41) — non-significant. Australia’s NHMRC71 concluded there is no good-quality evidence homeopathy is effective for any health condition.

Chiropractic / spinal manipulation for chronic low back pain: SMT vs. recommended therapies produces a pain reduction of −5.63 points on a 0–100 scale — statistically significant, clinically trivial.

A.7   Mental health treatments

Effect sizes in psychotherapy research depend critically on the comparison group.

Intervention Comparison Effect Source
Psychotherapy (general, depression) Waitlist / no treatment d=0.70–0.80 Smith & Glass72; Wampold 2019
Psychotherapy (depression) Active placebo d=0.20–0.30 Cuijpers et al.40
Bona fide therapy A vs. bona fide therapy B — d=0.00–0.20 (“Dodo bird”) Luborsky; Wampold
CBT for anxiety Waitlist d=1.23 Carpenter et al.73
CBT for anxiety Placebo g=0.56 (2008–2018); 0.24 (2017–2022) Carpenter et al.
Mindfulness-based programs Active controls SMD=0.30–0.38 Goyal et al.74 (JAMA)
Trauma-focused CBT, EMDR (PTSD) Waitlist d≈1.0+; NNT<4 Cusack et al.75
CBT-I (insomnia) Control g=0.57–0.98 van Straten et al.76 (2017)

The waitlist inflation problem. Waitlist controls typically show negative change over time as symptomatic clients wait for help they know is coming; this inflates the apparent effect of any intervention compared against them. Active placebo and treatment-as-usual comparisons, which are more honest counterfactuals, routinely yield effects half the size or smaller. Almost every flagship psychotherapy finding collapses by about 50–70% when the comparator moves from waitlist to active control.

A.8   Epidemiology & nutrition

Nutritional epidemiology is the field where the gap between individual studies and meta-analyses is largest. Schoenfeld and Ioannidis44 found that 80% of common cookbook ingredients had been linked to cancer in at least one study. Individual studies claimed median relative risks of 2.20 (increased-risk claims) or 0.52 (decreased-risk claims). Meta-analyses of the same associations yielded a median RR of 0.96, with interquartile range 0.85–1.10 — essentially null.

Exposure Outcome Effect Source
Smoking Lung cancer RR≈15–30 Established epidemiology
Mediterranean diet Composite CVD HR=0.70 PREDIMED
Mediterranean diet All-cause mortality HR=1.0 (null) PREDIMED
Processed meat (per 50g/day) Colorectal cancer RR=1.18 IARC meta
Coffee (3–5 cups/day) All-cause mortality RR=0.87 Meta-analyses
Sugar-sweetened beverages (per serving/day) Type 2 diabetes RR=1.27 Meta-analyses
Vitamin D supplementation Mortality, CVD, cancer ~Null VITAL trial; Bolland meta
Multivitamins All-cause mortality RR=0.98 (null) 21-RCT meta
Fish oil (EPA+DHA) MACE HR=0.99 (STRENGTH) vs. 0.75 (REDUCE-IT) Conflicting RCTs
Salt reduction (6 g/day) SBP in hypertensives −7.1 mmHg He & MacGregor
Exercise (vs. sedentary, meeting guidelines) All-cause mortality HR≈0.80 Multiple large cohorts
PM2.5 (per 10 μg/m³) All-cause mortality RR=1.08 WHO; Chen & Hoek77

Mendelian randomization has overturned many observational findings. The alcohol “J-curve” (observational RR = 0.75–0.85 for moderate drinking) was undone by MR studies showing monotonically increasing harm (OR = 1.27 per SD increase). Vitamin D’s extensive observational protective associations are mostly null in MR. HDL cholesterol’s inverse association with CVD proved non-causal. Where MR confirms causality — LDL → CHD, BMI → diabetes, smoking → cancer — concordance with RCTs strengthens confidence that the effect is genuinely causal.

A.9   Genetics

The central tension in genetics is between large aggregate heritability and minuscule per-variant effects. Polderman et al.78 meta-analyzed 17,804 traits across 2,748 twin publications and 14.6 million twin pairs, finding overall heritability of 49%. Yet individual genetic variants each explain less than 0.1% of variance for complex traits.

Trait Twin h² PGS R² Per-SNP contribution
Height 80–90% ~25–40% ~0.003% (12,111 SNPs in Yengo 2022)
Intelligence 50–80% ~10% <0.01%
BMI 40–70% ~8–9% <0.01%
Schizophrenia 60–80% ~7–18% liability <0.05%
Major depression 30–40% ~2–3% <0.01%
Educational attainment 40–60% ~10–15% <0.01%

The candidate gene era was a catastrophe. Border et al.79 definitively showed that the 18 most-studied candidate genes for depression — including 5-HTTLPR — are all null in adequately powered samples (N > 100,000). As a set, depression candidate genes were no more associated with depression than randomly selected non-candidate genes. Duncan and Keller80 found that 96% of novel candidate gene-by-environment interaction studies reported significant results, but only 27% of replication attempts did — consistent with the interpretation that most or all positive cG×E findings are type I errors.

A.10   Neuroscience

Marek et al.30, analyzing ~50,000 individuals across ABCD, HCP, and UK Biobank, found that the median reproducible brain-behavior correlation is r = 0.01, with the top 1% of effects at r > 0.06. Published small-sample neuroimaging studies (median N ≈ 25) routinely reported correlations of r = 0.20 to 0.80 — inflated by one to two orders of magnitude. Even at N = 1,000, half of statistically significant associations were inflated by at least 100%. Approximately N = 9,500 individuals are needed for 80% power to detect the largest 1% of brain-behavior effects.

Button et al.81 found the median statistical power of neuroscience studies to be about 21%. Szucs and Ioannidis82 examined 26,841 statistical records from 3,801 neuroscience and psychology papers and found median power of 12%, with significant results reporting median d = 0.93 while non-significant results sat at d = 0.24 — the fingerprint of massive selective inflation.

A.11   Economics & behavioral policy

Ioannidis, Stanley, and Doucouliagos83 analyzed 64,076 estimates from 6,700+ empirical studies across 159 economics research literatures. They found median statistical power of 18%, with roughly 80% of reported effects exaggerated; the typical inflation factor was 2×, and one-third of effects were inflated by a factor of four or more. Doucouliagos36 examined 22,000 partial correlations and found a median absolute partial r of 0.173.

The clearest demonstration of publication-to-reality shrinkage comes from DellaVigna and Linos22, who compared 26 academic nudge studies to 126 RCTs (covering 23 million individuals) run by the U.S. Office of Evaluation Sciences and the Behavioural Insights Team North America. Academic papers reported an average impact of 8.7 percentage points (a 33% increase over control groups). Government nudge units found 1.4 percentage points (an 8% increase) — a 6× gap, roughly 70% of which is attributable to selective publication.

Finding Effect Source
Auto-enrollment 401(k) participation 37% → 86% Madrian & Shea84
Opt-out vs. opt-in organ donation ~99% vs. low teens Johnson & Goldstein85
Default effects (meta-analytic) d=0.68, I²=98% Jachimowicz et al.86
Loss aversion coefficient λ (original) ~2.25 Kahneman & Tversky
Loss aversion λ (meta-analytic) 1.31 to 1.96 Brown et al.87; others
Financial literacy interventions on behavior ~0.1% variance (r≈0.03) Fernandes, Lynch & Netemeyer88
Financial literacy (updated meta) ~3× larger than above Kaiser et al.89
Minimum wage employment effect (bias-corrected) Close to zero Doucouliagos & Stanley90
Active labor market programs, short run ~Zero Card, Kluve & Weber91 (207 studies)
Active labor market programs, 2–3 years later Modestly positive Card, Kluve & Weber91

Three independently collected economics corpora — the Brodeur, Cook & Heyes198 “Methods Matter” file of causal-inference tests in top-25 econ journals (2015–2018), the Gechert–Havránek–Iršová–Kolcunová196 meta-analysis of the capital-labor elasticity σ (121 studies, 1961–2017), and the Havránek197 meta-analysis of the elasticity of intertemporal substitution (41 micro-panel studies, 1981–2012) — when placed on the same log N × log |d| axes as psychology’s statcheck corpus, produce a combined slope of -0.55 (r = -0.84, 272 papers, 75% above p < 0.05). This is essentially identical to psychology’s −0.45 (64% shrinkage per tenfold N) and close to the −0.5 theoretical slope a pure publication filter would produce. The three corpora span completely different research methods (reduced-form causal inference, structural parameter estimation, and household-panel consumption-Euler regression), different time periods (1961–2018), and different sample-size regimes (σ median N ≈ 44, Brodeur ≈ 4,800, EIS ≈ 5,700), yet all three produce the same boundary-hugging scatter. Economics is not a cleaner filter regime than psychology; it is the same filter regime. The 272 paper-level points appear as green circles in Figure 4; the three corpora’s filter derivations are documented in the data-extraction appendix.

A.12   Development economics

Vivalt41 compiled the AidGrade database of 15,024 estimates from 635 papers spanning 20 intervention types. The average standardized effect was about 0.12 SD. Heterogeneity was extreme — predicting the result of the next study from existing studies produced errors 99% as large as the effect itself. Even predicting the correct sign of an effect succeeded only 61% of the time. Government-implemented programs showed significantly smaller effects than academic or NGO implementations.

Intervention Effect Source
Microcredit (6-country RCTs) Modest, not transformative; ~7% business profit, could be zero Banerjee et al.92; Meager 2019
Cash transfers (GiveDirectly) +13% consumption; fiscal multiplier 2.4–2.7 Egger et al.93
Graduation approach +8% consumption, +15% assets, B/C ratio 379% by year 10 Banerjee et al.92 (Science); Bandiera et al.
Insecticide-treated bednets RR=0.83 child mortality; 5.6 lives/1,000/yr 22-RCT Cochrane
Deworming (short-run, Cochrane) Little effect on most outcomes Cochrane reviews
Deworming (20-year Kenya follow-up) +14% consumption, +13% earnings, 37% annualized return Hamory et al.23
Electrification (quasi-experimental) +40% income over 40 years Lipscomb et al.
Electrification (RCT) Null Lee, Miguel & Wolfram
International education RCTs Median d=0.10 on learning; 0.07 on access Evans & Yuan94

The electrification gap is instructive. Quasi-experimental designs yield estimates an order of magnitude larger than the RCT of the same intervention. This can be read in two ways: quasi-experimental studies are contaminated by confounding, or RCTs miss long-run general-equilibrium effects. The deworming long-run follow-up (20 years out) showing large effects when short-run studies showed little suggests that for interventions whose benefits accumulate over decades, short-run RCTs may genuinely understate impact. The lesson is not that RCTs are wrong but that the time horizon of measurement matters.

A.13   Education

Kraft37, analyzing 1,942 effect sizes from 747 education RCTs, found median effect 0.10 SD and weighted mean only 0.04 SD on standardized achievement. The 25th percentile was approximately zero (0.01 SD); the 75th was 0.25 SD. Pre-registered Department of Education studies showed a median of 0.03 SD. Kraft proposed recalibrated benchmarks: small < 0.05 SD, medium 0.05–0.20 SD, large ≥ 0.20 SD — making his “large” equivalent to Cohen’s “small.”

Lortie-Forgues and Inglis95 analyzed 141 large-scale education RCTs representing over 1.2 million students and found a mean effect of 0.06 SD. Only 23% of effects were statistically significant. The median Bayes factor was 0.56 — most results were literally uninformative.

Intervention Effect Source
Tutoring (pooled) d=0.29 Nickow, Oreopoulos & Quan96
Tutoring at scale (realistic) d=0.14–0.21 Kraft, Schueler & Falken97
Class-size reduction (Project STAR) d=0.15–0.25 Krueger; Finn & Achilles
Class size (Hoxby natural variation) ≈0, precision rules out > 0.03 SD Hoxby 2000
Perry Preschool, IQ at age 5 d=0.87 Original
Perry Preschool, IQ by age 14 ~0 (faded) Follow-ups
Perry Preschool, life outcomes age 40+ Large (+20pp HS grad, crime reduction) Heckman et al.
Head Start (cognitive, short-run) d=0.15–0.25, fades within 1–3y HSIS
Teacher value-added (per SD of teacher quality) 0.10–0.20 SD annual test scores Rivkin, Hanushek & Kain98; Chetty et al.99
Replace bottom-5% teacher with average teacher +$250K lifetime earnings per classroom Chetty et al.99
Hattie “Visible Learning” average d=0.40 — inflated 4–7× Kraft 2020 critique

A.14   Political science

Kalla and Broockman42 synthesized 49 field experiments on campaign contact in general elections and concluded that the best estimate of the average persuasive effect on vote choice is zero. The distribution of test statistics followed a normal distribution nearly perfectly, consistent with null effects. This held across mail, phone, canvassing, and advertising. Narrow exceptions exist: when candidates hold unusually unpopular positions, or when contact occurs very far before election day (though this early persuasion decays).

Get-out-the-vote effects are small but real. Door-to-door canvassing increases turnout by about 4.3 percentage points among those contacted (complier average causal effect), but since only 25–30% of targets are actually reached, the intent-to-treat effect is about 1 percentage point. Standard GOTV mail raises turnout by about 0.5 percentage points per piece. Social-pressure mailings are more effective at 2.3–2.85 percentage points. Robocalls and mass email show no statistically significant effects.

A.15   Criminology

Intervention Effect Source
Hot-spots policing d=0.132, no displacement Braga et al.43 (65 studies)
Juvenile delinquency interventions d=0.10–0.15 Lipsey & Cullen100
Drug courts (adult) Recidivism 50% → 38%; OR=1.65 Mitchell et al.
Scared Straight programs OR=1.68–1.72 (increase offending) Petrosino et al.101
Routine vs. researcher-led practice Routine is ~half the demonstration effect Multiple

A.16   Sociology & discrimination

Quillian et al.102 meta-analyzed all U.S. hiring audit studies from 1989–2015 and found whites received 36% more callbacks than African Americans, with no change over 25 years despite civil rights legislation, anti-discrimination law, and cultural shifts. Anti-Hispanic discrimination showed a modest decline.

Gender differences are mostly small. Hyde103 found 78% of gender effect sizes were d < 0.35 across 124 effects from 46 meta-analyses; math performance differences are d = −0.05 (trivial). Del Giudice104 showed that the multivariate Mahalanobis D for personality differences reaches 2.71 at the facet level — small univariate differences combine into substantial aggregate differences.

Values-affirmation writing interventions105 initially appeared to reduce the racial achievement gap by 40%. A well-powered registered replication106 found no effect, with precision sufficient to reject benefits larger than d = 0.10. Effectiveness appears highly context-dependent.

A.17   Organizational & management

Management quality explains 20–33% of cross-country and within-country productivity variation (Bloom and Van Reenen 2007; World Management Survey). An RCT providing management consulting to Indian textile firms improved productivity by 17% and profitability by about $325,000 per year107.

Leadership training (Lacerenza et al.108; 335 samples) shows δ = 0.63 for reactions, 0.73 for learning, 0.82 for behavioral transfer, 0.72 for results — among the largest intervention effects in applied psychology. General organizational training109 shows d ≈ 0.60–0.63. These effects may partly reflect measurement sensitivity to immediate post-training assessment.

Employee selection validity was substantially revised downward by Sackett et al.45. Corrected validity for cognitive ability dropped from r = 0.51 (Schmidt & Hunter110) to 0.31 — a 39% reduction. Structured interviews emerged as the best predictor at 0.42, dethroning cognitive ability from its 25-year position.

A.18   Marketing & advertising

Lewis and Rao46 showed that the R² of even a highly profitable ad campaign on individual-level sales is approximately 0.000005 — essentially zero. The standard deviation of individual-level sales is typically 10 times the mean. Experiments require over 10 million person-weeks to distinguish a profitable campaign from a null effect.

Blake, Nosko and Tadelis111 demonstrated this experimentally at eBay: when the company stopped bidding on branded search terms, virtually all traffic arrived organically. The causal effect of brand advertising was near zero. Sethuraman, Tellis and Briesch112 found mean short-term advertising elasticity of 0.12 (a 1% ad-spending increase yields 0.12% sales increase), with roughly half of campaigns showing no statistically significant effect.

A.19   Environmental & climate

Carbon pricing reduces emissions by 5–21% across schemes, shrinking to 4–15% after publication-bias correction (Döbbeling-Hildebrandt et al.47 (2024), Nature Communications). Sweden’s carbon tax reduced transport emissions by 11%113.

Energy conservation nudges (Allcott 2011 OPOWER): social-norm messaging reduces household electricity use by about 2.0% in the U.S. (range 1.4–3.3%), but only 0.7% in Germany. Pro-environmental behavior interventions produce very small effects: d = −0.09 for household climate action114. After publication-bias correction, climate mitigation nudge effects may approach zero.

WASH interventions experienced a dramatic downgrade. Three large RCTs (WASH Benefits in Bangladesh and Kenya; SHINE in Zimbabwe) found no effect on child linear growth and mixed effects on diarrhea — overturning historical meta-analytic estimates of 23–37% diarrhea reduction. Clean cookstoves similarly showed null health effects in the HAPIN trial despite achieving 66% reductions in PM2.5 exposure (birth weight +19.5g, non-significant; no pneumonia reduction).

A.20   Sports science

Intervention Effect Source
Caffeine on endurance performance d=0.33 (I²=0%) Meta-analyses
Beta-alanine (all uses) ES=0.18 Meta-analyses
Beta-alanine (0.5–10 min tasks) ES=0.50 Meta-analyses
Strength training on injury prevention RR≈0.32 (68% reduction) Lauersen et al.115
Stretching alone on injury prevention Null Multiple meta-analyses
Cold-water immersion on soreness SMD=−0.37 to −0.89 Meta-analyses
Compression garments on recovery g=0.40–0.49 Meta-analyses

A.21   Agriculture

Nitrogen fertilization increases crop yield by about 30–75% relative to no fertilization across global meta-analyses. Enhanced-efficiency fertilizers add another 8–10% on top of standard fertilizer. These effects are 5–50× larger than typical social-science intervention effects measured in comparable terms.

A.22   COVID-19

Intervention Effect Source
mRNA vaccines (phase 3, short-term) 94–95% RRR; ARR<1.2% Pfizer; Moderna trials
Dexamethasone (ventilated patients) 35% mortality reduction; NNT=8 RECOVERY trial
Medical/surgical masks RR=0.95 (NS) Cochrane116
Masks in Bangladesh NORM trial 9.5–11.5% reduction in seroprevalence Abaluck et al.
Part II — Cross-cutting patterns

A.23   Publication bias inflation factors by literature

Literature Published Bias-corrected Inflation
All Cochrane RCTs (median power 13%) Significant result at p=0.05 True effect ~3×
Economics (Stanley & Doucouliagos methods) Reported effect PET-PEESE corrected ~2×
Social psychology priming / ego depletion d=0.5–1.0 d=0–0.10 5–∞×
Growth mindset interventions d=0.08 d=0.02 ~4×
Nudges: academic vs. government scale 8.7 pp 1.4 pp 6×
Nutritional epidemiology (single studies vs. MA) RR=2.2 RR=0.96 ~23×
Candidate gene×environment 96% significant (novel) 27% significant (replications) ~3.6×
NHLBI CV trials, pre- vs. post-registration 57% positive (pre-2000) 8% positive (post-2000) ~7×
Antidepressants: FDA vs. published 94% positive in print 51% positive in FDA database ~1.8× positive-rate; ~32% effect-size inflation
Small-sample BWAS vs. adequately powered r=0.20–0.80 r=0.01–0.06 10–80×
Preclinical animal stroke studies Published effect Corrected effect ~31% inflation
Economics, pre- vs. non-pre-registered (implied) r=0.36 (schools of fish) r=0.16 ~2×

A.24   The many-analysts problem

Three landmark crowdsourced reanalyses demonstrate how much effect sizes and significance depend on analytical choices rather than the data alone.

Study Design Finding
Silberzahn et al.117 29 teams, same soccer red-card dataset ORs ranged 0.89–2.93; 69% significant, 31% not
Botvinik-Nezer et al.118 70 teams, same fMRI dataset No two identical workflows; map overlap near zero
Breznau et al.119 73 teams, same immigration data 1,261 unique models; results strongly negative to strongly positive
Orben & Przybylski120 Screens & wellbeing, specification curve 600M+ valid specifications; median β=−0.04

A.25   Observational → experimental reversals

Anglemyer et al.121 (2014 Cochrane review) found that on average, observational studies and RCTs produce similar estimates (ratio of ratios 1.04, 95% CI 0.89–1.21). But this average masks consequential discordances: observational associations routinely turn out to be confounded. Mendelian randomization studies have been particularly useful reality checks.

Claim Observational Experimental / MR
Moderate alcohol lowers mortality (J-curve) RR=0.75–0.85 Monotonic harm; OR=1.27 per SD
Vitamin D supplementation reduces mortality Protective ~Null (VITAL, Bolland; MR)
HDL cholesterol protects against CVD Strong inverse Non-causal (MR)
β-carotene, vitamin E, selenium Protective against cancer Null or harmful in RCTs
HRT protects against CVD Protective Harmful (WHI)
Rural electrification on income +40% over 40 years Null in RCT
LDL → CHD, BMI → T2D, smoking → cancer Causal Confirmed by MR/RCTs

A.26   WEIRD samples and generalization

Henrich, Heine, and Norenzayan122 documented that 96% of psychology research participants come from Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic societies representing 12% of the world’s population. WEIRD subjects are frequent outliers — Ultimatum Game offers (Western ~40% vs. 26% in some small-scale societies), susceptibility to visual illusions, self-concept independence. Effect sizes calibrated on WEIRD samples may not generalize globally.

In development economics, Vivalt41 found that geography has surprisingly little explanatory power for effect-size variation. A result from Kenya does not even generalize to Kenya very well — prediction errors are about 99% of actual effect sizes. Whatever drives heterogeneity in treatment effects operates at a finer grain than country or region.

A.27   The time trajectory of published effects

The decline effect — the phenomenon that published effects shrink over time as a field matures — is largely an artifact of reduced publication bias as replication becomes possible. Three data points:

  • NHLBI cardiovascular trials: 57% significant pre-ClinicalTrials.gov (2000), 8% significant afterward (Kaplan & Irvin26).
  • Dumas-Mallet et al.: initial highly-cited findings in biomedical research are subsequently refuted or substantially revised about 40% of the time.
  • Fanelli123: positive-result rates increased 22% from 1990 to 2007 across disciplines — not because science got better, but because publication pressures intensified.

When the world gives you large effects

Appendix A argued that the modal real effect is small. This appendix catalogs the exceptions — interventions that produce genuinely large effects, survive replication, and translate to field implementation without collapsing. The catalog is not long relative to the appendix that precedes it, and that is the point. Across the full range of things humans have tried to change about human beings, animals, ecosystems, and economies, the interventions that produce order-of-magnitude effects on outcomes we care about number in the dozens, not the thousands. Examining them closely reveals why: they share a specific structural signature, and most candidate interventions do not meet it.

The working definitions: an effect is “genuinely large” if (a) its magnitude sits at or above the 90th percentile of its field’s published effect-size distribution, (b) the point estimate is stable under high-quality replication or large-scale implementation, and (c) the bias-corrected estimate remains substantial after accounting for publication selection, small-sample inflation, and the winner’s curse. Most of the entries below clear those bars by wide margins. Several are so large that the normal machinery of effect-size statistics gives up — a d of 3 or a number-needed-to-treat of 2 is not a number, it is a therapeutic miracle that predates the tools invented to measure lesser interventions.

B.1   Infectious disease — antibiotics, vaccines, antivirals

The first and still the largest class of genuinely large effects is the control of infectious disease. An effective antimicrobial against a susceptible pathogen produces cure rates that approach the natural history of the untreated condition in its severity, with NNTs of 2 or 3 to prevent mortality in conditions that were otherwise reliably fatal.

Intervention Condition Effect Source / note
Penicillin Bacterial pneumonia, endocarditis Mortality ~90% → ~10% Historical pre/post 1940s
Streptomycin Tuberculous meningitis Mortality 100% → ~30% MRC 1948, first modern RCT
Smallpox vaccine Smallpox ~100% protection; disease eradicated 1980 WHO
Polio vaccine Paralytic polio US incidence 58,000/yr → ~0 Salk/Sabin era
Measles vaccine Measles 97% effective (2 doses); 60%+ global mortality reduction since 2000 WHO/CDC
HPV vaccine Cervical cancer (precursors) ~90% reduction in CIN2+ in vaccinated cohorts Swedish, UK register studies
