Blood pressure, test score, depression rating, vote share, GDP, body weight—every outcome behavioral and biomedical science measures is a sum or average over enormous numbers of underlying micro-events. The central limit theorem then squeezes the distribution of any such aggregate toward a bell with finite variance. Suppose this weren’t true. Then a single neuron firing or a single cell dividing could shift the whole outcome; your blood pressure this morning would be uncorrelated with your blood pressure yesterday; the class average on the retest would have no systematic relationship to the class average on the first test. Talking about “the mean” of anything would be nonsense, because the next measurement would wander anywhere. We know outcomes don’t behave this way because re-measuring the same person or the same group gives a similar number. The aggregation that makes re-measurement meaningful also fixes the variance and caps what any single input can do.

Systems dissipate. Biological, social, and mechanical systems lose energy to friction, noise, and entropy; perturbations decay, usually exponentially, with a timescale set by the system’s own structure. Suppose they didn’t. Then every cup of coffee you’ve ever drunk would still be moving your heart rate right now. Every argument you’ve ever had would still be raising your cortisol. Every warm summer day from your childhood would still be contributing to your current body temperature. Every teacher you had in third grade would still be adding a discrete amount to your SAT. The effect of a one-shot intervention would sum forever. It obviously doesn’t. You wake up in the morning roughly the same person you were yesterday, which means the system has quietly eaten almost everything that happened to it. The machinery that erases your personal history also erases most of the effect of a short-duration study intervention.

Homeostatic feedback. On top of passive dissipation, living and social systems maintain active negative-feedback loops around set points210. Body temperature, blood glucose, cortisol, clearing prices, wage levels, political equilibria—each is held in a narrow range by regulatory machinery that detects deviation and counteracts it. Suppose there were no homeostasis. The first time you skipped a meal, your blood glucose would have crashed to zero. The first cold day would have dropped your body temperature to ambient. The first bad week at work would have permanently lowered your mood by a standard deviation. A single missed dose of medication would not merely be suboptimal; it would be lethal. You are reading this sentence because your body and the society you live in are dense with restoring forces that undo most perturbations. Interventions have to overpower those forces. Most don’t.

Substrates are finite. A child has twenty-four hours in a day. A cell has finite ATP and membrane surface. A household has finite income. A country has finite tax base. Effects on an outcome are built out of the same substrate the outcome itself depends on, so the intervention cannot move the outcome by more than the substrate allows. Suppose substrates were unbounded. Then an extra hour of tutoring wouldn’t come out of something else the child was doing; you could add hours to the day until test scores hit any number you wanted. You could stack antidepressants until depression scores were arbitrarily negative. You could pile on exercise until resting heart rate was zero. Conservation says otherwise. Every intervention competes against the interventions already present, against the physical limits of the outcome scale, and against whatever else the substrate was being used for.

Equilibria absorb repeated interventions. Wherever agents interact repeatedly — markets, workplaces, classrooms, parliaments, households, doctor’s offices — the per-interaction effect has to be small, or the system would have moved to a corner and stayed there long ago. Suppose each interaction had a large effect. Then the first company to run a persuasive ad would own the market by the next quarter212. The first teacher who hit on a good lesson would have raised their students’ lifetime earnings by orders of magnitude above their peers. A single therapy session would cure or entrench any condition. A single argument would resolve or destroy any marriage. Every supposedly “viral” idea would actually go viral; politics would lurch from consensus to consensus each news cycle; stock prices would move to zero or infinity and stop.

Instead, these systems are visibly stable. Their parameters drift on timescales of years, not minutes, because every large shove gets met by countervailing shoves. Competitors copy the good pitch. Substitutes appear for the taxed cigarette. Counter-arguments form against the persuasive ad. Attention moves on from the viral post. Prices reprice. Per-interaction effects that look large under the microscope of a single study equilibrate against everyone else’s effects, and what the system settles into is close to what it was before. Most outcomes worth studying are embedded in such systems, which is why any pilot-scale effect decays as the intervention moves from the researcher’s lab into a world of adapting agents.

The exceptions prove the rule. Some outcomes do have effectively unbounded variance: earthquake magnitudes, financial crashes, pandemic cases, viral cascades, wealth, war deaths208,209,213. These are outcomes where a single patient zero produces millions of cases, one bank failure cascades into a global crash, one post goes viral, one weapon causes a war. Each of the first five properties breaks down for them. A single event determines the outcome (no averaging). No restoring force intervenes (no dissipation, no homeostasis). Shocks propagate through long-range dependence (no aggregation bound). The substrate is effectively unbounded over relevant horizons. That is the world in which single-study frequentist analysis is most dangerous, because the regularity assumptions it relies on are exactly the properties such outcomes violate. The rest of science — the part that studies mood, attitudes, test scores, behavior, physiology, prices — does not live in that world. Its outcomes are bounded by one or more of the five constraints, which is why its effects are small.

The outcomes behavioral and biomedical science actually measure sit inside all five of these constraints. Given that, the only remaining question is how small a cause acting on one of them has to be, which is what the eleven mechanisms below answer. Keeping the two layers distinct matters for reading the literature. A large effect on a power-law outcome (earthquakes, pandemic cases, viral cascades) is physically possible. A large effect on mood, attitudes, test scores, physiology, or behavior is not.

Why mechanisms acting on those outcomes have to be small

Given a bounded outcome, consider what a large mechanism would mean. If a single gene determined adult height, genetics would be a one-page paper. If a single teacher determined adult income, the achievement gap would track teacher rosters. If a single childhood experience determined personality, siblings would be identical. If a single conversation determined an attitude, no one would ever change their mind twice. None of these worlds exist. The mechanisms that actually move outcomes have to share a variance budget with thousands of others doing the same thing, and they do it through an estimator whose view of them is itself bounded.

Five mechanisms across two families make this precise. The first family is the variance budget itself: how much any single cause is permitted to claim when the outcome is produced by many of them, either in parallel or along a chain. The second family is what the estimator can physically see: the contrast between treatment and control, the noise added by measurement, the ceiling imposed by bounded scales. All five hold regardless of who runs the study or how carefully. Four of the five admit exact derivations collected at the end of this section.

Constraints on the effect-size budget

If an outcome has finite variance and is produced by many causes, those causes have to share the variance budget—so most of them have to be small. The same constraint applied along a causal chain instead of across parallel causes gives geometric decay in chain length. You cannot escape both at once: if the fan-in is narrow, the chain is usually long, and vice versa. These are the most physical of the eleven mechanisms in the sense that they hold regardless of who does the study or how it is run.

Polygenicity and the piranha bound

The variance budget for a bounded-variance outcome forces many-cause effects toward zero.

When a bounded-variance outcome is produced by k independent causes collectively explaining a share h² of the variance, the variance budget forces the average standardized effect to scale as O(k−1/2). You cannot pack many large, independent causes into the same bounded-variance outcome; they eat each other. For the outcome model

Y = Σi βi Xi + ε

with standardized mutually uncorrelated Xi and Var(Y) = 1, we have Σ βi² ≤ h² ≤ 1. Under equipartition βi² = h²/k, so the per-input standardized effect is |βi| = (h²/k)1/2 and the per-input Cohen’s d is roughly 2βi. The piranha theorem (Tosh et al. 2025) sharpens this: for unit-variance predictors X₁, …, X_p and outcome Y,

Σi Corr(Xi, Y)² ≤ 1 + 2 Σi < j |Corr(Xi, Xj)|.

If p predictors each claim correlation ≥ ρ with Y, either ρ ≲ 1/√p or the predictors are substantially mutually correlated. The “many large, independent effects” world is formally excluded.

DAG A. Polygenic fan-in. The unit-variance constraint on Y forces Σ βᵢ² ≤ 1, so that under any near-equipartition of causal contributions the typical |βᵢ| is of order k−1/2. Large independent effects “eat” the variance budget.

Concrete numbers. Adult height has h² ≈ 0.8 and Yengo et al. 2022 identified roughly 12,111 independent genome-wide-significant SNPs jointly explaining 40–45% of variance: the mean per-SNP R² is about 3 × 10−5, giving per-SNP |β| ≈ 0.006 and per-SNP d ≈ 0.01. Educational attainment (Lee et al. 2018) finds 1,271 lead SNPs with PGI R² ≈ 11–13%. Chabris et al. 2012 tested 12 candidate IQ genes in ∼10,000 subjects and replicated essentially none — the pre-GWAS candidate-gene era operated with effect-size priors off by two orders of magnitude. Brain-behavior associations collapse from r = 0.2–0.8 in small samples to r = 0.01 in adequately powered ones (Marek et al. 2022).

Implied ceiling. For a socially manipulable trait with h² ≈ 0.10–0.20 spread across k = 50–200 contributing mechanisms, the per-cause |d| is bounded at roughly 0.05–0.15. Szaszi, Higney & Gelman 2022 put the observed post-correction nudge average at d ≈ 0.08 — within a factor of two of the polygenicity prediction.

Tosh, Greengard, Goodrich, Gelman, Vehtari & Hsu 2025 28, Notices of the AMS; Boyle, Li & Pritchard 2017 153, Cell 169(7):1177–1186; Chabris, Lee, Cesarini, Benjamin & Laibson 2015 154, Current Directions in Psychological Science 24(4):304–312; Turkheimer 2000 155, Current Directions in Psychological Science 9(5):160–164; Yengo et al. 2022 29, Nature 610(7933):704–712; Marek et al. 2022 30, Nature 603(7902):654–660.

Mediation chains and geometric attenuation

Behavioral interventions travel through chains of intermediate steps; the total standardized effect is the product of the per-link path coefficients.

Treatments rarely act directly on the distal outcome of interest. They act on a proximal mediator, which influences a second mediator, which influences a third, which eventually influences the outcome. Under linearity the total standardized effect of the whole chain is the product of the per-link standardized path coefficients, and because each |βℓ| ≤ 1 (Cauchy–Schwarz on unit-variance variables), the product decays geometrically with chain length. Most famous behavioral interventions are implicitly 3- or 4-hop chains, and the algebra tells you medium-sized distal effects are nearly impossible.

δdistal = ∏ℓ = 1..n βℓ,   |ddistal| ≈ 2 · |∏ βℓ|

Pearl 2001’s natural indirect effect collapses to exactly β₁ β₂ in the linear no-interaction case. Composition with classical measurement error adds reliability attenuation as an additional multiplicative factor ∏ √rℓ. Fritz & MacKinnon 2007 showed that detecting the indirect effect ab with each path equal to a “small” 0.14 requires N ≈ 20,886 for 80% power — a quantitative proof that composed small effects are operationally invisible in typical samples.

DAG B. Four-link mediation chain. Under linearity, total standardized effect = β₁ β₂ β₃ β₄. With per-link coefficients between 0.3 and 0.4, the total shrinks below the “small” threshold.

Concrete numbers.

per-link β4-link productimplied dN/arm at 80%
0.20.00160.0031,600,000
0.30.00810.01662,500
0.40.02560.0516,200
0.50.06250.1251,000
0.70.24010.49565

Only when every link exceeds 0.5 do distal d's enter Cohen's “medium” range. A typical behavioral example: nudge → attention (β₁ = 0.30) → intention (β₂ = 0.35) → short-term behavior (β₃ = 0.40; Webb & Sheeran intention–behavior meta-analysis) → 6-month outcome (β₄ = 0.30). Product:  0.0126, implying d ≈ 0.025 and an 80%-power sample of N ≈ 25,000/arm.

Implied ceiling. With realistic per-link coefficients in [0.3, 0.4] and chain length n = 3–4, the mediation bound gives |ddistal| ≲ 0.02–0.13 — almost always below the d = 0.2 “small” threshold.

Pearl 2001 158, Proceedings of UAI-01, 411–420; MacKinnon, Lockwood, Hoffman, West & Sheets 2002 159, Psychological Methods 7(1):83–104; Loken & Gelman 2017 160, Science 355(6325):584–585.

What the estimator can actually see

A study does not measure an effect; it measures a contrast between an arm that got the treatment and an arm that did not, and if the control arm already has most of what the treatment adds, the contrast is small. Every instrument adds measurement noise that attenuates the underlying signal. And every bounded outcome — accuracy near 100%, mortality near 0% — imposes a hard ceiling on how far the treatment mean can move. None of these depend on journals or researchers. They depend on what you can physically see from the outside of a stochastic system.

The counterfactual gap against active controls

Effect sizes are properties of contrasts, not of treatments; the better the control, the smaller the gap.

The estimand τ = E[Y(1) − Y(0)] is a difference, and its magnitude depends on both arms. In mature domains the control arm receives most of the causally active ingredients — standard care, an existing teacher, a baseline reminder — so the contrast shrinks by subtraction. Write δT = μT − μ₀ and δC = μC − μ₀ for the treatment and control arms against a hypothetical no-intervention baseline μ₀:

dT vs C = (δT − δC) / σ ≤ dT vs 0 · (1 − ρ),  ρ = δC / δT.

In the Hernán–Robins DAG with a shared mechanism S activated by both arms and specific mechanisms δT, δC, the active-control contrast mechanically cancels the shared component.

DAG C. Active-control DAG. Both arms T and C share the mechanism S (expectancy, monitoring, clinician contact). The observed contrast (T vs C) cancels S's contribution; only the difference of the specific effects δT − δC survives.

Concrete numbers. DellaVigna & Linos 2022 (Econometrica 90(1):81–116): 126 RCTs at two US Nudge Units covering 23 million individuals, compared against the published academic nudge literature. Academic mean: 8.7 pp take-up increase (33.4% over control). Nudge-unit mean: 1.4 pp (8.0% over control). A 6.2× gap, of which DellaVigna and Linos attribute roughly 70% to publication selection under low power and the remainder to intervention-characteristic differences. Kraft 2020 shows that only ∼13% of ∼2,000 education RCT effect sizes on standardized achievement exceed d = 0.40; in his 2023 follow-up, 36% are below d = 0.05. Cipriani et al. 2018 (Lancet): antidepressant drug-vs-placebo SMDs ≈ 0.30, drug-vs-drug contrasts cluster near zero.

Implied ceiling. For light-touch policy interventions against realistic active counterfactuals, d ≲ 0.05–0.10. Kraft’s revised education-RCT benchmarks put “large” at d ≥ 0.20, not Cohen’s 0.80.

Freedman 1987 163, NEJM 317(3):141–145; DellaVigna & Linos 2022 22, Econometrica 90(1):81–116; Kraft 2020 164, Educational Researcher 49(4):241–253; Cipriani et al. 2018 59, Lancet 391(10128):1357–1366.

The signal-to-noise ceiling from measurement

Observed correlations are bounded by the reliabilities of the measurements that produced them — Spearman’s 1904 theorem, still binding.

Every observed correlation or standardized mean difference is bounded above by the reliabilities of the measures that produced it. With X = X* + eX and Y = Y* + eY (independent errors), reliability rXX = Var(X*)/Var(X):

Corr(X, Y) = Corr(X*, Y*) · √(rXX rYY),   dobs = dtrue · √rYY.

Loken & Gelman 2017 add the selection-interaction point: under conditioning on significance in small noisy samples, mean attenuation coexists with upward inflation of surviving estimates. Noise damages truth-telling bidirectionally.

DAG D. Classical measurement-error structure. Latent causes X*, Y* are contaminated by independent errors U_X, U_Y to produce observed X, Y. Spearman attenuation means the observed correlation is bounded by √(rXX rYY) times the true correlation.

Concrete numbers. Hedge, Powell & Sumner 2018 report test-retest ICCs for seven “robust” cognitive tasks: Stroop RT-cost 0.60–0.66, Flanker RT-cost 0.40, Posner/Navon effects near 0, go/no-go commission errors 0.76, SSRT ≈ 0.43. Elliott et al. 2020 meta-analyzed task-fMRI across 90 experiments (N = 1,008): mean ICC 0.397; 56% of values fall in the “poor” range below 0.40; only 20% reach “good/excellent.” With a brain measure at ICC 0.40 and a questionnaire at 0.80, the observed-correlation ceiling is √(0.40 · 0.80) ≈ 0.57 regardless of the underlying truth. The reliability paradox (Hedge et al.): the very property that makes Stroop and flanker robust at the group level — tiny between-subject variance — is what destroys their power for individual differences. The tasks used most often to operationalize cognitive control are the tasks worst suited to it.

Implied ceiling. For self-report outcomes (α ≈ 0.8), dobs ≤ 0.89 dtrue; for IAT (≈ 0.5), ≤ 0.71; for flanker (≈ 0.4), ≤ 0.63; for task-fMRI correlated with a 0.4-reliable behavioral measure, the correlation ceiling is 0.40, not 1.0. No amount of N fixes this — additional data pushes you toward the attenuated limit, not past it.

Spearman 1904 166, American Journal of Psychology 15(1):72–101; Hedge, Powell & Sumner 2018 167, Behavior Research Methods 50(3):1166–1186; Elliott et al. 2020 168, Psychological Science 31(7):792–806; Loken & Gelman 2017 160, Science 355(6325):584–585.

Ceiling and floor effects on bounded outcomes

If the outcome can’t go higher, the effect size can’t go higher.

When the outcome is bounded — a proportion in [0, 1], a Likert score in [1, 7], accuracy on an easy task, a reaction time above a physiological floor — the space of possible post-treatment means is mathematically compressed. A high baseline leaves no room for improvement, and under truncation the SD itself shrinks, so d = Δμ/σ is doubly bounded. For a dichotomous outcome with baseline p₀, Cohen’s h-statistic upper bound is

hmax(p₀) = π − 2 · arcsin(√p₀).

For a bounded continuous outcome with maximum M and baseline mean μ₀, the Bhatia–Davis inequality gives σ ≤ √(μ₀ (M − μ₀)).

Concrete numbers.

baseline p₀hmax
0.30≈1.98
0.50≈1.57
0.70≈1.16
0.90≈0.64
0.95≈0.45
0.99≈0.28

At p₀ = 0.95, the physically maximum improvement (p₁ → 1) produces h ≈ 0.45 — categorically below Cohen’s “large.” Halving mortality from 2% to 1% (a life saved per 100, an immense absolute gain) produces h ≈ 0.08 — “less than small.” The WAIS-IV caps at FSIQ ≈ 160, so any intervention tested on a gifted sample is mathematically prevented from producing medium-or-larger d's.

Implied ceiling. Depends on baseline; near-ceiling populations cap d in the 0.1–0.4 range regardless of treatment potency.

Cohen 1988 171, Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed.); Uttl 2005 172, Psychological Science 16(6):460–467.

What the research apparatus adds on top

So far the argument has stayed within physics: outcomes that have variance at all, and the bookkeeping that forces causes acting on those outcomes to be small. Real studies do not, however, report the bookkeeping; they report estimates that have passed through a research ecosystem. Six mechanisms describe what the ecosystem adds on top of the physics: two from the reporting filter, four from the ways a single site’s estimate fails to generalize. Two of the six admit exact derivations.

What the reporting filter does to the estimate

The first two families are physics. This one is not. Even when the underlying effect and the measurement apparatus are honest, the research ecosystem itself adds bias in predictable directions. Studies that reach the literature have passed a significance filter, which inflates the magnitude of the estimates it lets through. Participants who know they are being watched shift their behavior, which inflates the estimate a second time. Both biases attenuate at scale or under unobtrusive measurement, which is why nudges tested by government units produce smaller effects than nudges tested by academics. These bounds tell you not what the world allows, but what the current ecosystem is doing to what you see.

The winner's curse and regression to the mean

Conditioning an estimate on passing |t| > 1.96 inflates its magnitude, even with no journal selection.

Investigators apply a significance filter to themselves long before journals do. Conditioning an estimate on passing |t| > 1.96 biases its magnitude upward by an amount that depends only on the study’s statistical power. Gelman and Carlin 2014 formalize this as the Type M (magnitude) and Type S (sign) error framework. For θ̂ ∼ N(θ, s²) and δ = θ/s,

Power(δ) = Φ(δ − 1.96) + Φ(−δ − 1.96),
Type S(δ) = Φ(−δ − 1.96) / Power(δ),
E[|θ̂| | sig] = s · [φ(1.96 − δ) + φ(1.96 + δ) + δ (Φ(δ − 1.96) − Φ(−δ − 1.96))] / Power(δ).

The Type M ratio is E[|θ̂| | sig]/|θ|:

Powerδ = θ/sType SType M
0.060.300.1977.9
0.100.650.0453.7
0.201.140.0052.25
0.301.450.0011.81
0.501.96<10−41.41
0.802.80<10−61.12
DAG E. Publication-selection collider. Conditioning on S = 1 (passing significance) opens a collider path between true δ and noise ε via d̂, so E[|d̂| | sig] > |δ| for all δ.

Concrete numbers. The OSC 2015 empirical shrinkage from mean original r ≈ 0.403 to mean replication r ≈ 0.197 — a 2.05× ratio — is consistent with implied median power in the 15–20% range. The same factor-of-six DellaVigna–Linos shrinkage shows up again by a different mechanism, suggesting that structural shrinkage factors cluster in the same numerical range across very different sociologies.

The same mechanism repeats at longer timescales. An early extreme estimate, selected for publication precisely because it was extreme, is itself a draw from a distribution of δ̂’s that has a posterior mean much closer to the prior. Formally, for δ ∼ N(μ₀, τ²) and δ̂ | δ ∼ N(δ, σ²), the Bayes posterior is E[δ | δ̂] = μ₀ + [τ²/(τ² + σ²)] (δ̂ − μ₀). A replication draws δ̂rep from that posterior distribution and therefore regresses. The James–Stein result is the strictest statement of the same fact: for k ≥ 3 parameters, the MLE is dominated by any shrinkage estimator, even when the parameters are unrelated. Pereira, Horwitz and Ioannidis 2012 examined 85,002 Cochrane forest plots: of 8,239 with a very-large (OR ≥ 5) index trial, the median OR dropped to ≈1.6 when subsequent trials were added, and exactly one — ECMO for neonates — persisted as a very large effect on a mortality outcome. That’s 99.988% regression to the mean by count, and it is not the pathology; it is the arithmetic.

The decline effect observed across literatures (Schooler 2011, the “Proteus phenomenon”) is the same phenomenon stretched out over the publication history of a finding. Stanley, Carter and Doucouliagos 2018 surveyed 12,065 effect sizes across 200 meta-analyses, found median power at 36%, and found bias-corrected means typically 0.3–0.5× the unadjusted mean. The famous cases read the same way: Bargh, Chen and Burrows 1996’s elderly-priming walking-speed effect, originally d ≈ 0.5–0.9 on n = 30, collapsed to d ≈ 0 in Doyen et al. 2012’s infrared-timed replication on n = 120, with experimenter expectancy accounting for whatever signal remained. Every Type M ratio above row one of the table tells you this story in advance.

Implied ceiling. There is no upper bound on d̂ under selection — it grows without limit as power → α. The bound is on credibility: published d̂ at typical psychology power (≤ 0.2) should be divided by at least 2.25 to recover an honest point estimate; at power ≈ 0.06, divide by 8–10.

Gelman & Carlin 2014 169, Perspectives on Psychological Science 9(6):641–651; van Zwet & Cator 2021 31, Statistica Neerlandica 75(4):437–452; Ioannidis 2008 170, Epidemiology 19(5):640–648; Efron & Morris 1977 177, Scientific American 236(5):119–127; Ioannidis & Trikalinos 2005 178, Journal of Clinical Epidemiology 58(6):543–549; Pereira, Horwitz & Ioannidis 2012 179, JAMA 308(16):1676–1684; Schooler 2011 187, Nature 470:437; Stanley, Carter & Doucouliagos 2018 188, Psychological Bulletin 144(12):1325–1346; Bargh, Chen & Burrows 1996 189, JPSP 71(2):230–244; Doyen et al. 2012 190, PLoS ONE 7(1):e29081.

Hawthorne and demand effects washing out

Lab effects that are really “participants knowing they’re being watched” vanish at scale. The magnitude of this component is bounded.

Experimental estimates include a “research participation” component — subjects alter behavior because they know they are observed, or infer what the experimenter wants. At scale, in naturalistic environments, or under unobtrusive observation, this component vanishes. And it is bounded: de Quidt, Haushofer & Roth 2018 show that deliberately induced demand shifts behavior by only ≈0.13 SD on average across 11 classic tasks on MTurk. That’s a hard ceiling on how much of any lab effect can be Hawthorne artifact.

Concrete numbers. de Quidt et al.: average point bound on demand 0.13 SD (range ≈0.1–0.3); within-subject compliers shift up to 0.77 SD, but the average is 0.13. McCambridge, Witton & Elbourne 2014’s review of 19 Hawthorne studies concludes that “there is no single Hawthorne effect”: 12 of 19 showed a signal, but heterogeneity was pronounced and direction- and context-dependent. Orne 1962’s chilling demonstration: subjects told to add columns of two-digit numbers and tear up each answer sheet continued for 5.5 hours without complaint, assuming the pointlessness was itself the experiment.

Implied ceiling. Any published lab d < 0.15 is consistent with being entirely demand artifact. A published d = 0.30 leaves a true post-demand d ≈ 0.17; scaled unobtrusive estimates typically land below 0.10.

de Quidt, Haushofer & Roth 2018 193, American Economic Review 108(11):3266–3302; Orne 1962 194, American Psychologist 17(11):776–783; McCambridge, Witton & Elbourne 2014 195, Journal of Clinical Epidemiology 67(3):267–277.

Why pilots do not scale

The last family covers why the same intervention, run in a different place or at a different time, does not produce the same number. Real populations are heterogeneous; individual treatment effects vary, and the average the study reports is a specific average that a different population will not match. Group-level associations are not individual-level associations. Identical protocols run in different labs produce different point estimates by irreducible amounts. And the first reports of any effect tend to be larger than the stable mature version, partly because early estimates regress and partly because the easy versions of any problem get solved first. Three of these are physical properties of heterogeneity; the fourth (decline-over-time) is a mix of regression and sociology. All four are why a pilot that worked does not have to scale.

Heterogeneity absorbs the average

Interventions that work for some people and harm others can produce ATEs near zero with large individual effects underneath.

The average treatment effect a study reports is a summary of the individual treatment effect distribution across people, contexts, and moments. When that distribution has substantial dispersion, large positive effects in some subgroups cancel against null or negative effects in others, producing a small ATE even when the intervention is powerful for specific subpopulations. With τi ∼ (τ̄, στ²), outcome noise σ², and random assignment,

ATE = τ̄,   Var(ATÊ) = (2σ² + στ²)/n.

If τi = τ₀ + τ₁ Wi with E[W] = 0, the ATE = τ₀ is invariant under moderator symmetry while |τi| can be arbitrarily large — the ATE can be zero while every individual is materially affected. Detecting an interaction of the same magnitude as a main effect typically requires 16× the sample size (Gelman 2018).

DAG F. Moderation / effect modification. The X → Y edge’s coefficient depends on the level of the moderator W. The ATE is the population average of a heterogeneous individual effect.

Concrete numbers. Toy nudge scenario: d = 0.8 for 20% of the population, d = 0 for 60%, d = −0.3 for 20%. ATE = 0.10. E[|τi|] = 0.22. SD(τi) ≈ 0.37. Kenny & Judd 2019’s critique: the field’s preference for single mega-trials over distributed replication is strategically misguided. With any genuine between-site τ, no single study — however large — generalizes. The fix is distributed multi-site work, not bigger single sites.

Implied ceiling. When στ is comparable to |τ̄|, the ATE can shrink to 0.1 while individual |di| averages 0.4 — a factor-of-four absorption. For most behavioral interventions, ATE ceilings of d ≈ 0.1–0.3 coexist with much larger CATEs for responsive subgroups.

Bryan, Tipton & Yeager 2021 173, Nature Human Behaviour 5(8):980–989; Kenny & Judd 2019 174, Psychological Methods 24(5):578–589; Olsson-Collentine, Wicherts & van Assen 2020 175, Psychological Bulletin 146(10):922–940; Gelman 2018 176, blog post, Statistical Modeling.

Compositional and ecological effects

Group-level correlations aren’t individual-level correlations. The same data can give opposite answers.

Correlations computed at group level can differ in magnitude or sign from correlations at individual level. Designs that mix within- and between-person variance produce a published effect that is a weighted mixture of two structurally different covariances. Interventions targeted on the basis of ecological associations therefore hit a much smaller — sometimes oppositely-signed — individual-level parameter. Decomposing Yij and Xij into between- and within-cluster components,

Cov(Y, X) = Cov(αj, βj) + Ej[Cov(ε, η | j)].

Simpson’s paradox occurs whenever those two correlations differ in sign.

Concrete numbers. Robinson 1950 on 1930 US census data: state-level correlation between percent foreign-born and literacy was +0.53; individual-level correlation was −0.11. Modern analogues include Gelman’s red-state / blue-state income–vote inversion and the Berkeley 1973 admissions paradox (Bickel, Hammel & O’Connell 1975): the university appeared to discriminate against women overall while every department admitted women at equal or higher rates. Molenaar’s ergodicity theorem: between-person equals within-person only if the process is stationary and homogeneous — conditions almost never met in psychology.

Implied ceiling. An ecological correlation r = 0.5 (d ≈ 1.15) with within-cluster r = 0.1 yields individual-level d ≈ 0.2; under sign-flip it yields zero or negative. Rule of thumb: multiply the naive d by the between-to-total variance ratio, typically 0.05–0.20 in cluster-sampled social data.

Robinson 1950 183, American Sociological Review 15(3):351–357; Kievit, Frankenhuis, Waldorp & Borsboom 2013 184, Frontiers in Psychology 4:513; Molenaar 2004 185, Measurement 2(4):201–218; Bickel, Hammel & O’Connell 1975 186, Science 187(4175):398–404.

Site-to-site heterogeneity and voltage drop at scale

Even with identical pre-registered protocols, effect sizes differ across sites by irreducible amounts.

Even under identical pre-registered protocols, true effects vary across sites due to latent moderators (population, language, experimenter, stimuli micro-variation). Random-effects meta-analysis partitions variance into sampling σ² and between-study τ². With τ on the d scale of 0.10, a meta-analytic mean of 0.20 has a 95% prediction interval of roughly [0, 0.40] — about one site in six finds d ≤ 0 by pure heterogeneity, with no mischief required.

Concrete numbers. Many Labs 2 (Klein et al. 2018): 28 studies × 125 samples, N = 15,305 in 36 countries. Median original d = 0.60; median replication d = 0.15 — a fourfold shrinkage. 57% of replication effects fell below d = 0.20; 32% pointed opposite the original direction. The headline finding inverts the WEIRD critique: variability in effect sizes was attributable more to which effect was studied than to which sample or setting ran it. Many Labs 5 (Ebersole et al. 2020): protocol-level heterogeneity τ = 0.034 even after author-advised revisions; paying experts to perfect procedures bought almost nothing.

The same heterogeneity shows up at larger scales as a voltage drop between pilot and policy. Pilots enjoy selected compliant sites, charismatic protocol-dedicated implementers, tight fidelity monitoring, selected cooperative subjects, and short time horizons. At scale, fidelity erodes, marginal sites and implementers are less effective, and general-equilibrium feedbacks arrive. Formalizing this as ATEscale = ATEpilot · (1 − ϕ) · ρpop − γ · GE, where ϕ is fidelity loss, ρpop is population representativeness, and GE is general-equilibrium feedback: every term is < 1 in practice, and their product is small. The DellaVigna and Linos 6.2× academic-to-nudge-unit gap is a clean empirical measurement of this product. Bloom 1984’s famous “2 sigma” tutoring result of d ≈ 2.0 becomes d ≈ 0.10–0.25 in modern daily-tutoring RCTs on standardized tests, a 10–20× voltage drop on what was the most-studied effect in education research. The Many Labs heterogeneity is what voltage drop looks like at zero scale-up friction; real-world scale-up adds friction on top.

Implied ceiling. With typical Many Labs τ ≈ 0.10, any single replication’s expected d is about the ML2 median of 0.15. For effects with genuine moderator heterogeneity (τ > 0.20), single-site honest d is capped around 0.10–0.20 because half the random-effects mass lies below the point estimate.

Klein et al. 2018 20, AMPPS 1(4):443–490; Ebersole et al. 2020 191, AMPPS 3(3):309–331; Open Science Collaboration 2015 19, Science 349(6251):aac4716; DellaVigna & Linos 2022 22, Econometrica 90(1):81–116; Al-Ubaydli, List & Suskind 2017 192, AER P&P 107(5):282–286; Kraft 2020 164, Educational Researcher 49(4):241–253; Bloom 1984 215, Educational Researcher 13(6):4–16.

Equilibration as low-hanging fruit disappears

Over time, within a literature and across fields, the easy wins are harvested first; what remains is harder.

Effect sizes within a literature shrink as studies accumulate (the Gehr–Weiss–Porzsolt “fading of reported effectiveness”) and across fields as easy wins are exhausted (Bloom, Jones, Van Reenen & Webb’s “ideas are getting harder to find”). The first combines regression to the mean, publication-bias decay, and methodological improvement; the second is the structural exhaustion of frontier discoveries. Both push the expected magnitude of newly discoverable effects downward over time.

Concrete numbers. Gehr, Weiss & Porzsolt 2006 show LDL-C reduction by pravastatin declined by ≈0.5 percentage points per year over 15 years of trials — a ≈30% relative decline in reported effectiveness. Fanelli, Costas & Ioannidis 2017 analyzed 3,042 meta-analyses across all disciplines and found a negative meta-regression slope on publication year across most fields. Bloom et al. 2020: sustaining Moore’s Law now takes ~18× more researchers than in 1970; agricultural yields show a ~4× decline in research productivity; pharma new-drug approvals per R&D dollar have declined roughly monotonically since the 1960s.

Implied ceiling. Within a mature literature: published meta-analytic d at the ~5-study mark is typically 2–3× larger than the eventual stable d, so an early d = 0.5 corresponds to long-run d ≈ 0.15–0.25.

Gehr, Weiss & Porzsolt 2006 180, BMC Medical Research Methodology 6:25; Pereira, Horwitz & Ioannidis 2012 179, JAMA 308(16):1676–1684; Bloom, Jones, Van Reenen & Webb 2020 182, American Economic Review 110(4):1104–1144.

The empirical evidence: three literatures, one fact

Everything above has been a-priori: physics and bookkeeping that says the world cannot produce large effects on the outcomes science actually measures. If the published literature reports them anyway, the gap between the a-priori expected size and the published size has a name — inflation — and three separate literatures measure it three different ways. Each uses a different method. Each arrives at a similar shrinkage. That is what convergence looks like.

Published effects shrink on direct replication. The Open Science Collaboration attempted to replicate 100 psychology studies and found replication effect sizes roughly half the originals (r = 0.40 → r = 0.20), with only 36% passing standard significance thresholds19. Many Labs 2 replicated 28 effects across 125 samples and found mean d collapse from 0.60 to 0.15; 57% of replications landed below d = 0.20 and 32% pointed opposite the original20. The SCORE project, covering 12 disciplines, found replication effects roughly 40% of originals21. Specific famous effects — ego depletion, facial feedback, elderly-walking priming, money priming, warm-cup-warm-judgment — shrank by 75–100%.

Effects shrink from lab to field implementation. DellaVigna and Linos compared academic nudge effects to the same nudges run by large government units and found average academic effects of 8.7 percentage points versus field effects of 1.4 — a factor of 622. Hamory et al. 202123 found twenty-year deworming effects smaller than initial estimates implied. The Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology found replicated preclinical effects 85% smaller than originals24. The 5% preclinical-to-clinical translation rate214 is this shrinkage at its most extreme.

Preregistered studies produce smaller effects than unregistered ones. Registered Reports yield positive results 44% of the time versus 96% for standard articles (Scheel, Schijen & Lakens 2021)17. NHLBI trials post-ClinicalTrials.gov produced positive results at 8% versus 57% before registration (Kaplan & Irvin 2015)26. FDA-registered antidepressant effects were 32% smaller than the published literature implied (Turner et al. 2008)27.

Three shrinkages, three literatures, three methodologies. They are all the same thing. The publication process selects for large, surprising, statistically significant findings; the true distribution of effects is centered much closer to zero; the gap between the two is what all three literatures independently measure. Once you see this, you stop being surprised that replications fail, that pilots do not scale, and that preregistration shrinks the literature. These are the same fact. The engine is selection, and its strength is the distance between the published world and the real one.

Why fixing the filter isn’t enough

Suppose the publication filter vanished tomorrow. Every study got published, positive or null; preregistration was universal; selective reporting ended. Would the small-N literature then be informative?

Mostly no. A small-N study is an instrument with two relevant thresholds, and small-N advocates routinely confuse them. For a balanced two-sample t-test with total sample size N, a result crosses p < 0.05 (two-sided) when the observed d exceeds roughly 3.92/√N: d ≈ 0.62 at N = 40, d ≈ 0.39 at N = 100, d ≈ 0.28 at N = 200. That is the publication-filter threshold, and it is what the literature above has been talking about. The other threshold is harder: to have 80% power against a real effect, the true d has to be about 5.60/√N—roughly d = 0.89 at N = 40, d = 0.56 at N = 100, d = 0.40 at N = 200. The study can cross significance even when the true effect is much smaller than what it can reliably detect—because crossing requires only that the noise push the observed d above the filter threshold. The literature is dominated by upper-tail draws.

The true distribution of effects in most behavioral and biomedical domains sits in d ∈ [0.01, 0.25], with mass concentrated well below 0.20. A typical small-N study is, by construction, blind to nearly everything real. It can only detect effects larger than the ones that physically exist on its target outcome.

A post-filter small-N literature would then consist of three things. A sea of nulls, because most real effects are smaller than the minimum detectable. A sprinkling of true discoveries, where a genuinely large effect happens to be present—a pharmacologically powerful intervention on a tractable endpoint. A stream of false positives at the α rate, inflated by the same Type M factor the current literature suffers from, because the only way a small-N study detects anything is through the upper tail of its sampling distribution.

And the nulls are less informative than they look. A preregistered null from a small-N study does not tell us the true effect is zero. It tells us only that the observed d in that particular sample didn’t cross the publication-filter threshold—which, for N = 40, means observed d < 0.62. Because the study had only 23% power against a true d = 0.4 and just 58% against d = 0.7, a null here is consistent with a true effect anywhere from zero up through territory we’d normally call “large.” We already thought the true d was probably under 0.2. The null result narrows that belief not at all. It is uninformative in the same way that asking whether your thumb is heavier than a bowling ball is uninformative about the weight of your thumb.

The publishing filter is therefore not the primary disease. The primary disease is the mismatch between the resolution of the instrument and the size of the object being measured. Fixing the filter is necessary but not sufficient. Without also fixing sample size, we would trade today’s literature of inflated positives for a literature of meaningless nulls plus occasional false positives plus rare-and-accurate large effects.

There is one exception. Small-N studies are the right instrument for the outcomes where large effects are physically possible—earthquake magnitudes, financial crashes, pandemic cascades, and a handful of pharmacologically tractable clinical endpoints—and the wrong instrument everywhere else.

And one escape. A thousand preregistered small-N studies on the same effect, coordinated through a Many Labs or SCORE-style protocol, pool into an effective N large enough to resolve d = 0.05. Per-study small-N is nearly information-free for most outcomes. Coordinated-many-labs small-N is the instrument those outcomes need. The replication crisis is not a filter crisis. It is a resolution crisis that the filter made visible.

Putting it together: the compound effect-size model

The mechanisms above are not independent nuisances to be patched one at a time. They are structural constraints that compound multiplicatively. The same underlying physics — bounded variance, noisy measurement, finite samples, complex causal structure — shows up in different places as different bounds: piranha-theorem bounds on many-cause effects, geometric decay along causal chains, Spearman ceilings on measured effects, Type M inflation on selected estimates, voltage drops on scaled interventions. Compounding polygenicity with mediation gives

|d| ≲ k−1/2 · β̄n.

With k = 100 contributing causes, chain length n = 3, and mean per-link coefficient β̄ = 0.4: |d| ≲ 0.006. That is the true baseline for distal behavioral effects of single interventions in complex systems. A published effect of d = 0.50 on such an outcome implies either the causal structure is much simpler than assumed, or the estimate is off by nearly two orders of magnitude.

Several cross-cutting numbers recur and are worth keeping on hand: per-SNP R² ≈ 10−5, task-fMRI median ICC ≈ 0.40, Stroop ICC ≈ 0.60, median published-psychology power ≈ 36%, OSC original-to-replication shrinkage ≈ 2×, DellaVigna–Linos academic-to-nudge-unit shrinkage ≈ 6×, Many Labs 2 median replication d ≈ 0.15, de Quidt demand bound ≈ 0.13 SD, Kraft education-RCT “large” threshold d = 0.20. Cohen’s original 0.2 / 0.5 / 0.8 benchmarks, derived in a 1988 textbook from unbounded idealizations, are empirically unreachable in most of the outcomes behavioral science actually measures.

The conceptual upshot is a restatement of the opening contradiction. Small effect sizes are not a pathology to be fixed; they are the only possibility. The aggregated, dissipated, homeostatic, equilibrating, substrate-bounded outcomes behavioral and biomedical science measures cannot contain large effects on most interventions, because a world in which they did would not be the world any of us lives in. The crisis is the mismatch between that world and a publication culture calibrated on unreachable priors. Fixing it does not require heroic new methods. It requires honesty: acknowledging that the effects on most outcomes we study are real but small, that the piranha theorem forbids many of them from being large, and that the statistical tools and editorial norms of the field need to match the actual size of what they are measuring. We have always lived in the world where small-N studies don’t matter. The question is only whether we read the literature like we do.

Derivations for the mathematically inclined

This subsection collects the formal derivations behind the six mechanisms with exact (rather than empirical) bounds. Readers who want the intuition without the algebra can skip it. Notation: Var(·) is variance, Corr(·,·) correlation, Φ the standard normal CDF, φ the standard normal density.

D1. The polygenicity bound: why many independent causes must be small

Consider the linear outcome model with standardized inputs X1, …, Xk that are mutually uncorrelated, and independent noise ε:

Y = Σi = 1..k βi Xi + ε

Take Var(Xi) = 1 and Var(Y) = 1. Because the Xi are mutually uncorrelated and independent of ε,

Var(Y) = Σi βi2 Var(Xi) + Var(ε) = Σi βi2 + σε2 = 1.

Define the total heritability / explained share h2 ≡ Σi βi2, so h2 ≤ 1. Under approximate equipartition, βi2 = h2/k, giving

|βi| = (h2/k)1/2 = O(k−1/2).

Translating to Cohen’s d for a per-input contrast: for binary Xi ∈ {0, 1} with population share p, a unit increment raises E[Y] by βi and the two-group standardized mean difference is di = βi/√(p(1 − p)). For p = 0.5 this gives di ≈ 2 βi = O(k−1/2).

Sharpening: the piranha theorem. Tosh, Greengard, Goodrich, Gelman, Vehtari & Hsu 2025 prove that for unit-variance random variables X1, …, Xp, Y (no independence assumption required),

Σi = 1..p Corr(Xi, Y)2 ≤ 1 + 2 Σi < j |Corr(Xi, Xj)|.

Proof sketch: write W = Σi Corr(Xi, Y) Xi and apply Cauchy–Schwarz to Cov(W, Y) using Var(W) ≤ 1 + 2 Σi < j |Corr(Xi, Xj)|. Consequence: if p predictors all claim correlation ≥ ρ with Y, then either ρ2 ≲ 1/p (so ρ ≲ p−1/2) or the predictors are substantially mutually correlated. Both escape routes force at least one of “few large effects” or “many small effects” to be true.

D2. Mediation attenuation via path tracing

Consider a two-link chain with standardized variables:

M = β1 X + eM,     Y = β2 M + eY,

with eM ⟂ X, eY ⟂ (X, M), and Var(X) = Var(M) = Var(Y) = 1. Then

Cov(X, Y) = Cov(X, β2 M + eY) = β2 Cov(X, M) = β2 · β1.

Since all variables are unit-variance, Corr(X, Y) = β1 β2. Inductively extending to a chain of length n,

Corr(V0, Vn) = ∏ℓ = 1..n βℓ.

Each |βℓ| ≤ 1 follows from unit-variance: 1 = Var(Vℓ) = βℓ2 Var(Vℓ − 1) + Var(eℓ) ≥ βℓ2. The product ∏ βℓ therefore decays geometrically with chain length when |βℓ| < 1.

Pearl’s natural indirect effect. Pearl (2001, UAI) generalizes this to nonlinear and interacting mediators. For a cause X, mediator M, outcome Y, the natural indirect effect is

NIE(x, x*) = E[Y(x, M(x*))] − E[Y(x*, M(x*))],

where Y(x, m) is the potential outcome under X = x and M = m. In the linear no-interaction case this collapses to β1 β2. Identifying the NIE nonparametrically requires cross-world independence assumptions (Robins & Richardson 2010) that are not empirically testable, but the product-law attenuation is robust to departures from linearity as long as mediators are not strongly antagonistic.

Composition with measurement error. If each mediator is measured with reliability rℓ < 1, the observed product is

β̂total = (∏ℓ √rℓ) · (∏ℓ βℓ).

A 4-link chain of measures each with r = 0.8 loses an additional factor of 0.64, roughly doubling the power cost.

D3. Spearman’s reliability ceiling

Classical test theory posits observed measures as true score plus independent error:

X = X* + eX,    Y = Y* + eY,

with eX ⟂ X*, eY ⟂ Y*, eX ⟂ eY. Reliability is rXX = Var(X*)/Var(X), with the analogous rYY for Y. Then:

Cov(X, Y) = Cov(X* + eX, Y* + eY) = Cov(X*, Y*)

(all cross-terms with errors are zero by independence). And:

Var(X) = Var(X*)/rXX,    Var(Y) = Var(Y*)/rYY.

Therefore:

Corr(X, Y) = Cov(X*, Y*) / √(Var(X) Var(Y)) = Corr(X*, Y*) · √(rXX rYY).

Because |Corr(X*, Y*)| ≤ 1,

|Corr(X, Y)| ≤ √(rXX rYY).

For a Cohen’s d on a binary predictor with reliable assignment (rXX = 1) and outcome reliability rYY: dobs = dtrue · √rYY. A 0.4-reliable outcome caps dobs at 0.63 · dtrue; extraordinary amounts of data converge on this attenuated limit rather than recovering dtrue. Recovery requires reliability-adjusted estimation (errors-in-variables regression, structural equation models), not more n.

D4. Type M and Type S errors under the significance filter

Let θ̂ ∼ N(θ, s2) be a normal point estimate, and suppose we condition on passing the two-sided 5% significance threshold |θ̂| > 1.96 s. Define the standardized true effect δ = θ/s. Power at this effect size is

Power(δ) = P(|θ̂| > 1.96 s | θ) = Φ(δ − 1.96) + Φ(−δ − 1.96).

The Type S error is the probability of getting the wrong sign given significance:

Type S(δ) = P(sign(θ̂) ≠ sign(θ) | |θ̂| > 1.96 s) = Φ(−δ − 1.96) / Power(δ).

The Type M ratio is the expected magnitude inflation of the surviving estimate:

Type M(δ) = E[|θ̂| | sig] / |θ|.

Computing the conditional expectation: for θ̂ = θ + s Z with Z ∼ N(0, 1), the significance region is {Z > 1.96 − δ} ∪ {Z < −1.96 − δ}. Using the standard truncated-normal identity E[Z | Z > a] = φ(a)/(1 − Φ(a)),

E[|θ̂| | sig] = s · [φ(1.96 − δ) + φ(1.96 + δ) + δ · (Φ(δ − 1.96) − Φ(−δ − 1.96))] / Power(δ).

At low power the first two terms (the noise pushing low-δ estimates past threshold in either direction) dominate over |θ|, producing severe Type M inflation. Gelman’s retrodesign R function computes all three quantities:

retrodesign <- function(A, s, alpha = .05, df = Inf, n.sims = 10000) {
  z     <- qt(1 - alpha/2, df)
  p.hi  <- 1 - pt(z - A/s, df)
  p.lo  <- pt(-z - A/s, df)
  power <- p.hi + p.lo
  typeS <- p.lo / power
  estimate     <- A + s * rt(n.sims, df)
  significant  <- abs(estimate) > s * z
  exaggeration <- mean(abs(estimate)[significant]) / A
  list(power = power, typeS = typeS, exaggeration = exaggeration)
}
# Durante et al. 2013 ovulation voting, claimed d = 0.30, power = 0.06:
retrodesign(A = 0.30, s = 1.0)
# power = 0.060, typeS = 0.197, exaggeration ≈ 9.7

D5. Ceilings on Cohen’s d for bounded outcomes

Dichotomous outcomes. For a binary outcome with baseline rate p0 and post-treatment rate p1, Cohen’s h (the standardized effect measure appropriate for proportions) is

h = 2 [arcsin(√p1) − arcsin(√p0)].

Maximizing over p1 ∈ [0, 1] at fixed p0 gives p1 = 1, so

hmax(p0) = π − 2 arcsin(√p0).

This is a monotonically decreasing function of p0; at p0 = 0.95, hmax ≈ 0.45, categorically below Cohen’s “large” threshold of 0.80.

Bounded continuous outcomes. For an outcome with maximum value M, baseline mean μ0 and SD σ,

dmax = (M − μ0) / σ.

But σ is itself bounded above by the Bhatia–Davis inequality: for X ∈ [0, M] with mean μ0,

Var(X) ≤ μ0 (M − μ0).

The two bounds together compress the feasible d for bounded outcomes. On a Likert scale [1, 7] with baseline μ0 = 6.2 and σ = 0.8, a realistic ceiling μ1 ≤ 6.5 gives d ≤ 0.38 — regardless of treatment strength.

D6. Heterogeneity variance decomposition

For random treatment assignment Ti ∈ {0, 1} and potential outcomes Yi(0), Yi(1), the individual treatment effect is τi = Yi(1) − Yi(0) with mean τ̄ and variance στ2. Assuming the treatment and control variances of Y(T) are both σ2, the difference-in-means estimator is

τ̂ = Ȳ̅1 − Ȳ̅0,    E[τ̂] = τ̄ = ATE.

Its sampling variance under random assignment at balanced n/arm:

Var(τ̂) = (σ2/n) · 2 + στ2/n = (2σ2 + στ2)/n.

The στ2/n term (the heterogeneity of the treatment effect itself) is typically ignored in power calculations but can dominate when στ ≈ |τ̄|.

Interactions and moderation. Suppose τi = τ0 + τ1 Wi with moderator Wi, Var(W) = σW2, E[W] = 0. The ATE is τ0 and the variance of the individual effect is τ12 σW2. Under moderator symmetry the ATE is invariant while the distribution of individual effects can be arbitrarily wide.

To detect an interaction effect of magnitude τ1 requires approximately 16× the sample size needed to detect a main effect of the same magnitude. The ratio follows from Var(τ̂1) ≈ 4 · Var(τ̂0) at equipartitioned moderator levels, plus the usual power-scaling relation n ∝ 1/δ2.

Random-effects prediction interval. For meta-analysis with between-study heterogeneity τ2 and within-study variance σi2, a new study’s effect is drawn from N(μ, τ2), and the 95% prediction interval is

μ̂ ± tk − 2, 0.975 · √(τ̂2 + SE(μ̂)2).

At μ̂ = 0.20 and τ = 0.10, the prediction interval is approximately [0, 0.40] — a meaningful share of new sites will return τ̂ ≤ 0 under the null hypothesis that the meta-analytic mean is correct. Failed replications are predicted, not surprising.

These six derivations are the backbone. The remaining mechanisms — winner’s curse, ceilings, equilibration, compositional effects, site-to-site heterogeneity, and demand artefacts — are empirical observations or applications of the same statistical machinery, not new mathematical identities.
